proposals.title: Spectral-Resolvent Criteria for Feedback Stabilizability of Non-Analytic C0-Semigroups
proposals.motivation: The feedback stabilization theory for infinite-dimensional linear systems is well-developed for analytic (parabolic) semigroups, where smoothing properties simplify analysis. However, many critical models in wave propagation, transport, and delay systems generate only strongly continuous, non-analytic semigroups, lacking this smoothing. Pre-2026, necessary and sufficient conditions for exponential stabilizability in this broad class remain fragmented, often relying on system-specific arguments rather than a unified spectral framework.
proposals.hypothesis: For a linear system with a non-analytic C0-semigroup generator, exponential stabilizability by bounded feedback is determined by a precise spectral condition linking the uncontrolled system's spectrum and resolvent growth to the structure of the observation and control operators.
proposals.method: We will classify non-analytic generators (e.g., hyperbolic, delay) by their spectral properties and semigroup type. Using detailed resolvent estimates and spectral distribution analysis, we will derive necessary conditions for stabilizability from the Hautus test and spectral mapping theorems. The constructive design will synthesize feedback laws via a spectral decomposition approach for systems with Riesz spectral operators and a Lyapunov-based method for more general cases, explicitly leveraging resolvent bounds to compensate for the lack of smoothing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified, spectral-resolvent framework for assessing and achieving feedback stabilization for a major class of infinite-dimensional systems beyond the parabolic case. It will yield verifiable, operator-theoretic criteria for control engineers and contribute new constructive feedback design methods for wave-like and delay dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The approach moves beyond the parabolic paradigm by directly tackling the core challenge of non-analyticity, linking stabilizability to resolvent growth rates rather than assuming instantaneous smoothing—a non-obvious connection given the predominance of analytic semigroup theory in control.

proposals.title: Feedback Stabilizability of Non-Parabolic Linear Infinite-Dimensional Systems: A Spectral and Semigroup Approach
proposals.motivation: The classical theory of feedback stabilization for linear infinite-dimensional systems is well-developed for parabolic-type systems, where the system operator generates an analytic semigroup. Many important applications, however, involve systems of non-parabolic type, such as certain coupled hyperbolic-parabolic systems, neutral systems, or systems with essential spectrum on the imaginary axis. Pre-2026, a general framework for the stabilizability of such broader classes, particularly leveraging gradient-like structures, is lacking. This work aims to bridge this gap, extending the applicability of feedback control theory to a wider range of physical and biological models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a significant class of non-parabolic linear systems, a suitable decomposition of the state space and a spectral analysis of the system generator can reveal conditions under which gradient-based feedback laws, or their generalizations, achieve exponential input-to-state stability (ISS) or asymptotic stabilization.
proposals.method: The approach will leverage the decomposition of the system operator's spectrum into its point and essential components, drawing on pre-2026 semigroup theory. We will first characterize non-parabolic systems by the properties of their generated C0-semigroup (e.g., differentiability, compactness). The core method involves constructing a Lyapunov functional tailored to the spectral structure, potentially using a modal decomposition or a perturbation argument from a known stable parabolic core. Specific algorithmic conditions for stabilizability will be derived by studying the solutions to the associated operator Lyapunov equation under non-standard spectral assumptions, linking to the 'gradient' and 'stationary distributions' themes from Sontag's recent work.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a rigorous set of sufficient conditions for the feedback stabilizability of linear infinite-dimensional systems beyond the parabolic regime. It will extend the theoretical foundation for control design in applications like flexible structures with damping and certain interconnected network dynamics. The results will also offer new insights into the interplay between gradient flow structures, spectral theory, and stabilization in abstract linear systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond the analytic semigroup framework that dominates the literature, systematically addressing stabilization for systems whose semigroups lack smoothing properties. It connects the spectral and geometric properties of non-parabolic generators to the construction of stabilizing feedbacks, a direction not fully explored pre-2026.

proposals.title: Operator-Theoretic Lyapunov Functions for Stability of Time-Varying and Nonlinear Infinite-Dimensional Systems
proposals.motivation: Classical stability results for linear time-invariant (LTI) systems, such as the equivalence between internal stability and input-to-state stability (ISS), rely on spectral properties of constant operators. In modern applications like adaptive control, switched systems, and neural networks with time-varying weights, these operators become time-dependent or state-dependent. Pre-2026, a core challenge is extending these fundamental guarantees beyond the LTI setting without resorting to restrictive assumptions like arbitrarily slow variation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a broad class of time-varying and nonlinear operator systems, key stability properties (e.g., ISS) can be certified by constructing a suitable time-varying or state-dependent operator-valued Lyapunov function, provided the system's nonlinearities satisfy sector conditions and the time variation is structured (e.g., in a slowly varying or persistent excitation sense).
proposals.method: We will adapt Lyapunov's direct method to an operator-theoretic framework. First, we will formulate candidate Lyapunov functions as quadratic forms defined by time-varying, positive-definite operator solutions to associated operator differential inequalities or equations, generalizing the algebraic Lyapunov equation. Second, we will employ dissipativity theory and integral quadratic constraints to handle nonlinearities. Third, we will derive verifiable sufficient conditions (e.g., on the derivative of the Lyapunov operator or the spectral properties of its generator) using tools from evolution families and perturbation theory for linear operators. The analysis will be grounded in the study of distributions of system trajectories and stationary behaviors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, Lyapunov-based methodology for proving stability of infinite-dimensional and operator-based systems with time-varying or nonlinear dynamics, directly extending the reach of classical linear systems theory. It will yield new, computationally tractable criteria for verifying the ISS property in applications like training dynamics of neural networks and adaptive control of distributed parameter systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit fusion of classical Lyapunov construction from nonlinear control with the functional-analytic treatment of time-varying linear operators and sector-bounded nonlinearities, moving beyond common Lyapunov function searches for switched systems to a more general, dynamically varying operator framework.

proposals.title: Stability and Input-to-State Stability of Gradient Flows for Systems with Time-Varying or Nonlinear Operators
proposals.motivation: The analysis of gradient flows for optimization, particularly in machine learning and systems biology, has largely focused on time-invariant linear operators. However, real-world systems, such as neural networks with adaptive connections or biological networks with feedback, often involve time-varying or nonlinear dynamics. Extending stability guarantees like the Input-to-State Stability (ISS) property to these more general classes is crucial for ensuring robustness in learning algorithms and understanding complex biological gradients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core ISS result for gradient flows of single-layer networks with linear activations can be generalized to systems where the operator is time-varying (e.g., A(t)) or belongs to a specific class of memoryless nonlinearities, provided the operators satisfy certain persistence of excitation or sector-bound conditions.
proposals.method: We will employ a Lyapunov-based framework, constructing candidate Lyapunov functions that incorporate the time-varying or nonlinear structure. For time-varying linear operators A(t), we will analyze the associated differential Lyapunov equation and employ tools from the theory of linear time-varying systems and persistence of excitation. For nonlinear operators, we will use incremental passivity and sector conditions, leveraging dissipativity theory. Numerical studies will involve simulating gradient flows for systems with randomly generated time-varying parameters or common nonlinearities (e.g., saturations) to validate the theoretical bounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous stability framework for gradient-based learning in non-stationary or inherently nonlinear environments. It will yield verifiable sufficient conditions for ISS, extending the theoretical toolkit for the analysis of adaptive systems and robust optimization algorithms beyond the classical linear, time-invariant setting.
proposals.novelty: While ISS for gradient systems is established for constant linear operators, the extension to time-varying and nonlinear cases is non-trivial and largely unexplored. The novelty lies in synthesizing tools from control theory (persistence of excitation, dissipativity) with optimization theory to handle these more complex, yet practically relevant, dynamical structures.

proposals.title: A Unified Symbolic-Numeric Procedure for Nonlinear Stabilizability Analysis and Verified Controller Synthesis
proposals.motivation: The design of stabilizing controllers for nonlinear systems remains a fundamental challenge in control theory, often requiring separate, non-constructive analyses for stabilizability and controller synthesis. While constructive methods like control Lyapunov functions (CLFs) exist, a unified computational pipeline that rigorously tests necessary conditions, automatically constructs certificates, and synthesizes verifiable controllers is lacking, hindering the reliable automation of nonlinear control design.
proposals.hypothesis: A computational procedure integrating Fattorini's condition for necessary stabilizability with automated CLF construction and Sontag-type feedback synthesis can reliably produce, for broad classes of nonlinear systems, either a proof of non-stabilizability or a formally verified stabilizing controller with a stability margin.
proposals.method: The procedure first employs symbolic-numeric methods to check Fattorini's necessary condition. For polynomial systems, it uses sum-of-squares (SOS) programming or linear programming to search for a polynomial CLF. For non-polynomial systems, it parameterizes a CLF candidate via a neural network, training it using a loss function derived from the CLF inequality. Upon finding a CLF, it synthesizes a continuous controller using Sontag's universal formula or a pointwise min-norm optimization. Finally, it employs interval arithmetic or satisfiability modulo theories (SMT) solvers for formal verification of closed-loop stability and robustness margins.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first end-to-end algorithmic framework that bridges nonlinear stabilizability analysis and constructive controller synthesis with formal verification guarantees. It will deliver open-source software that advances the automation of nonlinear control design, making rigorous methods more accessible for applications in robotics and systems biology.
proposals.novelty: The tight integration of a necessary stabilizability test within an automated synthesis loop is novel, as is the hybrid use of SOS for polynomials and neural CLFs for general systems, followed by formal verification, within a single procedure.

proposals.title: A Network-Theoretic Framework for Diagnosing Nonlinear Stabilizability via Fattorini's Criterion
proposals.motivation: Verifying the stabilizability of high-dimensional nonlinear systems is a fundamental challenge in control theory. While Fattorini's Lie algebraic criterion provides a systematic check, it yields a binary answer, offering no insight into which system interactions are critical or how to redesign a controller if the condition fails. This limits its utility for the analysis and design of complex systems in synthetic biology or robotics, where diagnostic feedback is essential.
proposals.hypothesis: By representing the Lie algebraic conditions of Fattorini's criterion as a weighted, structured control network, its stabilizability can be diagnosed via sensitivity analysis and modular decomposition algorithms from computational biology, revealing critical subsystems and bracket interactions.
proposals.method: We will encode the Lie bracket generation tree from Fattorini's procedure as a directed hypergraph, where nodes are vector fields and hyperedges represent bracket operations. Using algorithms for sensitivity analysis (akin to Metabolic Control Analysis) and modular decomposition (inspired by methods for gene regulatory networks), we will quantify the contribution of each component to the overall rank condition. This will be implemented in a computational tool that interfaces with existing symbolic algebra packages for Lie bracket computation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a formally grounded, diagnostic tool for nonlinear control design that moves beyond a yes/no answer to stabilizability. It will provide a new methodology for controller architecture selection and highlight system-theoretic bottlenecks. The fusion of control-theoretic Lie algebras with network analysis creates a novel bridge between mathematical control theory and computational systems biology.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in treating the abstract Lie algebraic condition of Fattorini's criterion as a quantifiable, analyzable network. The application of pathway sensitivity algorithms to a purely mathematical controllability condition, for the purpose of diagnostic feedback in controller design, is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Explicit Finite-Dimensional Feedback for Parabolic PDEs via Balanced Truncation and Gain Scheduling
proposals.motivation: Stabilizing parabolic PDEs often yields infinite-dimensional feedback laws, which are challenging to implement in practice. While model reduction is used for simulation, its rigorous application to construct explicit, implementable stabilizing controllers remains underexplored. This work bridges this gap by adapting a proven aerospace control paradigm to infinite-dimensional systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A low-order, explicitly computable state-feedback law, derived via balanced truncation of the linearized parabolic system and scheduled with system parameters, can provably stabilize the original infinite-dimensional system.
proposals.method: We will linearize the parabolic PDE around a parameterized equilibrium profile. For the resulting linear system, we will apply output projection and balanced truncation, specifically tailored for the analytic semigroup structure, to obtain a minimal, stable finite-dimensional ODE. An explicit LQR feedback gain will be computed for this reduced model. This gain will then be parameterized as a continuous function of the equilibrium parameter, creating a gain-scheduled, finite-dimensional control law for the full PDE.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a concrete, computationally tractable procedure for designing explicit stabilizing controllers for parabolic systems. It will provide a rigorous theoretical framework connecting model reduction fidelity to closed-loop stability guarantees, advancing the practical implementation of PDE control theory.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic translation of the aerospace gain-scheduling workflow to parabolic PDEs and the rigorous stability analysis of the resulting explicit finite-dimensional controller, which moves beyond abstract existence results to constructive design.

proposals.title: Input-to-State Stability for Nonlinear Systems via Bounded Geometric Multiplicity of the Linearization
proposals.motivation: Traditional Input-to-State Stability (ISS) analysis for nonlinear systems often relies on linearization or restrictive small-gain conditions, which can fail for systems with significant nonlinearities or complex center/unstable manifold structures. A pre-2026 gap exists in linking manageable linear spectral data directly to robust nonlinear stability guarantees without resorting to local approximations or overly conservative conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly bounding the geometric multiplicities of the eigenvalues of the system's linearization provides a structural constraint that limits the topological complexity of the nonlinear flow, enabling the construction of nonlinear ISS-Lyapunov functions and facilitating the application of ISS small-gain theorems for a broader class of systems.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework combining geometric control theory and nonlinear stability analysis. First, we will characterize how bounded geometric multiplicity restricts the dimension and embedding of center/unstable manifolds. Using this, we will construct piecewise, non-quadratic Lyapunov function candidates via techniques like Zubov's method or density functions, explicitly incorporating the multiplicity bound. The analysis will integrate with ISS small-gain formulations for interconnected systems, where the bounded multiplicity constraint simplifies the verification of the gain conditions. Numerical validation will employ algorithms for computing invariant manifolds and verifying dissipation inequalities on sampled state distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new sufficient condition for ISS based on linear spectral geometry, extending the applicability of ISS theory to highly nonlinear systems where linearization is inconclusive. It will provide a systematic method to translate finite-dimensional linear algebraic data into guarantees for infinite-time nonlinear behavior, bridging a gap between linear spectral theory and global nonlinear stability.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in using a purely linear algebraic invariant (geometric multiplicity) as a key enabler for *nonlinear* ISS analysis, bypassing the need for a stable linearization or explicit knowledge of the nonlinear terms' bounds, which is distinct from traditional Lyapunov or small-gain approaches.

proposals.title: Robust Observability and Stabilization of Nonlinear Stationary PDEs via Unique Continuation and H-infinity Synthesis
proposals.motivation: The control of infinite-dimensional systems governed by nonlinear PDEs, such as those modeling fluid flows or structural vibrations, is critically limited by two challenges: the need to reconstruct the full state from sparse, noisy sensor data, and the requirement for controllers to remain stable despite significant model uncertainty. Pre-2026, unique continuation theory provides powerful tools for state determinability, while H-infinity synthesis offers robust performance guarantees, but these frameworks remain largely disconnected. A unified theory is needed to enable the design of certifiably resilient control systems for complex physical processes.
proposals.hypothesis: The unique continuation property for a nominal linearized PDE can be leveraged to design a finite-dimensional observer whose error dynamics, when coupled with the nonlinear perturbation, admit a robust H-infinity or mu-synthesis controller that guarantees closed-loop stability and performance.
proposals.method: We will first establish sufficient conditions for a nonlinear PDE to satisfy a robust unique continuation property using Carleman estimates and harmonic analysis techniques, treating nonlinearities as bounded disturbances. This property will inform the placement of a finite set of sensors. We will then construct a finite-dimensional Galerkin approximation of the system and formulate the combined observer-controller synthesis problem as a linear fractional transformation (LFT). The robust controller will be synthesized using mu-tools, explicitly accounting for the structured uncertainty from the model reduction error and the bounded nonlinear perturbations. The analysis will employ dissipation inequalities and small-gain arguments to certify performance for the infinite-dimensional system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, computationally tractable framework for co-designing state estimators and stabilizing controllers for a class of nonlinear stationary PDEs with hard performance guarantees. It will yield explicit, verifiable conditions under which a finite-dimensional controller reliably stabilizes an infinite-dimensional plant, bridging a significant gap between PDE theory and practical robust control.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious fusion lies in formally connecting the harmonic analysis of unique continuation—a property about deterministic PDE solutions—with the stochastic and worst-case uncertainty descriptions of H-infinity/mu-synthesis, creating a new pathway for robust control design grounded in the intrinsic observability of the governing equations.

proposals.title: Globally Valid Robust Control for Nonlinear Systems via Structured Feedback Linearization and μ-Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Robust control for nonlinear systems often relies on local linearization, limiting guarantees to a neighborhood of an equilibrium. While exact feedback linearization offers a global coordinate transformation, the resulting linear model inherits a complex, structured uncertainty description from the original nonlinear dynamics. Current methods lack a systematic synthesis framework that exploits this structure to provide non-conservative, globally valid robustness guarantees across the transformed operating envelope.
proposals.hypothesis: By rigorously characterizing the uncertainty structure induced by the feedback linearization process and applying structured singular value (μ) synthesis to the transformed linear model, one can synthesize a nonlinear controller that guarantees robust stability and performance for the original plant across the entire region where the linearizing diffeomorphism is valid.
proposals.method: We will first apply exact feedback linearization to a class of nonlinear affine systems, deriving the transformed linear model and the associated uncertainty structure (e.g., parametric, dynamic) stemming from imperfect cancellation and modeling errors. This structure will be cast into a Linear Fractional Transformation (LFT) framework. We will then employ μ-synthesis via the D-K iteration algorithm, a pre-2026 standard, to design a controller for this uncertain linear model. Finally, the synthesized linear controller will be mapped back through the inverse coordinate transformation to obtain the final nonlinear control law, with stability analysis conducted using Input-to-State Stability (ISS) tools for interconnected systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled synthesis pipeline for nonlinear robust control that delivers guarantees beyond a local operating point. It will yield a concrete computational method linking geometric nonlinear control and structured linear robust control. The result is a controller with certified disturbance attenuation and stability for the nonlinear plant under significant model uncertainty.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit fusion of exact feedback linearization's global perspective with the rigorous, structured robustness analysis of μ-synthesis, moving beyond ad-hoc applications or local HJI solutions to provide a systematic design with non-local guarantees.

proposals.title: Robust Stabilization of Nonlinear Gradient Systems via Dissipativity-Based H-infinity Synthesis
proposals.motivation: The stabilization of nonlinear systems, particularly those arising from gradient flows in optimization and machine learning, is often analyzed under idealized conditions. Real-world perturbations and model uncertainties can destabilize such systems, compromising performance and safety. Pre-2026 robust control theory, specifically H-infinity and mu-synthesis, provides powerful frameworks for linear systems, but their direct application to significant nonlinearities remains a challenge, limiting their use in modern computational and biological networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by representing nonlinear perturbations as structured, dynamic uncertainties within a dissipative systems framework, we can synthesize robust stabilizing feedback controllers using convex optimization derived from H-infinity criteria, even when the nominal system is nonlinear.
proposals.method: The approach will first embed the nonlinear gradient system (e.g., from neural network training dynamics) into a linear fractional transformation (LFT) framework, isolating the nonlinearities as a structured uncertainty block. We will then formulate the stabilization problem as an H-infinity control synthesis for the linear nominal part, subject to integral quadratic constraints (IQCs) derived from the sector bounds and monotonicity properties of the nonlinearities. The synthesis will be performed using linear matrix inequality (LMI) solvers, and the resulting controller's performance will be validated numerically on benchmark problems, including stabilizing stationary distributions in stochastic gene regulatory network models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled methodology for certifying robust stability in nonlinear gradient and dynamical systems common in machine learning and systems biology. It will extend the applicability of classical robust control tools to a broader class of nonlinear problems, yielding computationally tractable design procedures. The results will offer new guarantees for the reliability of feedback mechanisms in uncertain networked systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic fusion of dissipativity and IQC analysis—tools used in Sontag's prior work on ISS properties—with H-infinity synthesis to handle *nonlinear* perturbations, moving beyond the typical small-gain or linearization approaches prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Minimal Control Dimension for Global Stabilization of Nonlinear Systems: A Geometric and Probabilistic Analysis
proposals.motivation: A fundamental question in control theory is understanding the minimal resources required to achieve a control objective. While the dimension of the state space often dictates controller complexity, the intrinsic lower bound on the dimension of the control input needed for global stabilization of general nonlinear systems remains poorly characterized. This problem is critical for the design of parsimonious and efficient control architectures in complex networks and engineered systems, where actuator placement and dimensionality are constrained.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a broad class of nonlinear systems with multiple isolated equilibria or complex omega-limit sets, there exists a non-trivial, topology-driven lower bound on the control dimension necessary for global asymptotic stabilization, which can be derived from the system's gradient-like structure and the geometry of its chain-recurrent set.
proposals.method: We will employ a hybrid geometric-probabilistic approach. First, we will use Conley index theory and tools from dynamical systems to characterize obstructions to stabilization (e.g., non-existence of immersions) that imply dimensional requirements. Second, we will formulate a stochastic optimization problem over stationary distributions of controlled diffusions to probe minimal actuator configurations. The analysis will leverage gradient and quasi-gradient system structures, with computational validation on benchmark nonlinear systems using numerical bifurcation and control synthesis tools.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel theoretical framework linking the minimal control dimension to the topological and dynamical invariants of the uncontrolled system. It will provide verifiable necessary conditions for stabilizability, moving beyond generic controllability results. The findings will offer practical guidelines for minimal actuator design in complex systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing Sontag's recent work on gradient flows, ISS properties, and obstructions from omega-limit sets to derive dimension bounds, a connection not explicitly made in prior stabilization literature. The combination of topological obstruction theory with a probabilistic study of stationary distributions for this purpose is non-standard.

proposals.title: Finite-Element Discretizations and Their Impact on Controllability Criteria for Infinite-Dimensional Bilinear Systems
proposals.motivation: The analysis and control of infinite-dimensional systems, such as those governed by PDEs, often rely on finite-dimensional approximations for computation and implementation. However, pre-2026 theory lacks a complete understanding of how specific discretization schemes, like finite-element methods, preserve or distort fundamental system properties like controllability. This gap is critical for ensuring the reliability of control designs derived from approximate models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a class of bilinear distributed parameter systems, a specific finite-element discretization scheme will preserve the exact controllability property of the original system in the limit, but that the rate of convergence depends explicitly on the interplay between the discretization's approximation properties and the system's spectral characteristics.
proposals.method: We will focus on a canonical bilinear parabolic PDE system. The method involves: 1) Constructing a Galerkin finite-element approximation, leading to a finite-dimensional bilinear ODE system. 2) Analyzing the controllability Gramian of the discretized system and comparing it to the infinite-dimensional counterpart using operator perturbation theory. 3) Employing numerical algebraic geometry tools to study the stationary distributions and reachable sets of the approximate system, quantifying deviations from the original system's dynamics as a function of mesh refinement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, quantitative framework for assessing when a discretized model is 'controllability-preserving.' It will yield explicit error bounds linking discretization parameters to controllability metrics, offering practical guidelines for control engineers. The results will bridge numerical analysis and systems theory for a relevant class of nonlinear infinite-dimensional systems.
proposals.novelty: While discretization of linear systems is well-studied, the analysis of how discretization affects the controllability of *bilinear* infinite-dimensional systems is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in combining finite-element error analysis with the study of controllability Gramians and stationary distributions for this nonlinear class, moving beyond purely linear or spectral approximations.

proposals.title: Robustness Certificates for Biological Feedback Networks via Input-to-State Stability and Stationary Distributions
proposals.motivation: Complex biological networks, such as gene regulatory or signaling pathways, maintain function despite internal stochasticity and external perturbations. Pre-2026 systems biology often uses dynamical systems models but lacks rigorous, scalable tools from control theory to quantify their robustness to structured perturbations and noise. Bridging this gap is crucial for predicting network failure modes and designing synthetic circuits.
proposals.hypothesis: The Input-to-State Stability (ISS) framework, combined with analysis of stationary distributions for stochastic dynamics, can provide computable, non-conservative bounds on a network's output deviations in response to persistent disturbances and parametric variations.
proposals.method: We will model biological feedback networks as nonlinear dynamical systems with disturbance inputs. The core approach involves: 1) Deriving ISS-Lyapunov functions for network motifs, leveraging recent work on gradient systems and linear activations to find constructive conditions. 2) For stochastic components, we will analyze the Fokker-Planck equation to characterize stationary distributions of key species under noise, linking distributional shifts to ISS gains. 3) We will develop computational algorithms using sum-of-squares programming to automate the search for ISS certificates for larger, modular networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new control-theoretic methodology to certify the robustness of biological networks, moving beyond linearization and simulation. It will yield explicit, quantitative bounds on admissible perturbations and predict how noise shapes long-term network states, offering tools for both analysis and design in systems biology.
proposals.novelty: While ISS is established in engineering control, its systematic application to stochastic biological network models with a focus on connecting ISS gains to stationary distributions is novel. This integrates Sontag's recent themes on ISS for gradient systems and the study of dynamic distributions into a unified robustness analysis framework.

proposals.title: Stability and Convergence Analysis of Gradient Flows for Neural Networks with Saturating Activations via Input-to-State Stability Frameworks
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Sontag and collaborators has established Input-to-State Stability (ISS) guarantees for gradient flows of single-layer networks with linear activations. However, practical neural networks universally employ non-linear, saturating activation functions (e.g., tanh, sigmoid), whose bounded, non-additive responses fundamentally alter the system's dynamics. A rigorous stability theory for these realistic, non-linear network responses is critically lacking, limiting the formal understanding of training dynamics and generalization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the ISS framework can be extended to gradient systems arising from networks with saturating activations by treating the saturation non-linearity as a bounded disturbance to a nominal linear-like dynamics, leading to quantifiable stability and convergence rates in function space.
proposals.method: We will model the training dynamics of a single hidden-layer network with saturating activations as a perturbed version of the previously analyzed linear-activation gradient flow. Using ISS and small-gain theorem techniques from non-linear control theory, we will derive sufficient conditions on network architecture (width, initialization) and data distributions that ensure stability. The analysis will employ Lyapunov functions constructed from the network's parameters and will study the stationary distributions of the resulting non-linear dynamical system to characterize convergence basins.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal ISS guarantees for gradient-based training of neural networks with saturating non-linearities, bridging a significant gap between classical control theory and modern machine learning. It will yield practical, verifiable conditions for training stability and offer new insights into the role of activation function choice on optimization dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While ISS for linear-activation networks is established, extending this to saturating non-linearities is non-trivial due to the loss of global linear structure. The novelty lies in the system-theoretic treatment of saturation as a structured perturbation and the use of stationary distribution analysis for these non-linear, non-convex flows.

proposals.title: Lyapunov Function Characterizations for Input-to-Output Stability and Related Notions
proposals.motivation: Input-to-Output Stability (IOS) and its variants (e.g., output-Lagrange stability, output asymptotic gain) are fundamental for analyzing the input-output behavior of nonlinear control systems, particularly in network and biological contexts. While Lyapunov conditions for Input-to-State Stability (ISS) are well-established, analogous necessary and sufficient Lyapunov characterizations for these output-centric stability notions remain incomplete, limiting the analysis and design of systems where only partial state information is relevant.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a unified framework of dissipativity-type inequalities, involving both the system state and the measured output, can provide necessary and sufficient conditions for the full suite of IOS-related properties.
proposals.method: We will employ a systems-theoretic approach, building on the ISS Lyapunov function formalism and recent work on omega-limit sets and gradient systems. The core method involves: 1) Formulating candidate Lyapunov functions that are functions of the state but whose decay conditions are expressed via the output map. 2) Using converse Lyapunov theorems, leveraging techniques from the analysis of stationary distributions and attractor dynamics, to establish necessity. 3) Applying these characterizations to case studies, including gradient-like systems (e.g., neural networks with linear activations) to verify the conditions and interpret the results in terms of convergence of observable quantities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a complete and practically verifiable Lyapunov toolbox for IOS and its variants, closing a significant theoretical gap. It will enable the direct Lyapunov-based analysis of output stability in complex networks and large-scale systems, with direct applications in systems biology and machine learning where internal states are high-dimensional but relevant outputs are low-dimensional.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in deriving necessary *and* sufficient Lyapunov conditions specifically tailored for output stability properties, moving beyond the sufficient-only conditions or state-based ISS frameworks prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Convex Parameterizations for Nonlinear Control via Koopman Lifting and Stationary Gradient Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Convex parameterizations of controllers, such as those based on Youla or system-level parameterization, are foundational for linear control synthesis, enabling efficient optimization with stability guarantees. Extending these benefits to nonlinear systems remains a major open challenge, limiting the application of modern convex optimization tools to complex, data-driven nonlinear control problems. A principled extension would significantly advance our ability to design certifiably stable and performant controllers for nonlinear dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: For classes of nonlinear systems with well-defined Koopman operator embeddings or gradient-like structure, a convex parameterization of stabilizing controllers can be constructed by lifting the problem to a higher-dimensional linear representation of the dynamics, where established convex system-level parameterizations can be applied.
proposals.method: We will first focus on systems admitting a finite-dimensional Koopman-invariant subspace, where the nonlinear dynamics become linear in the lifted coordinates. For this lifted linear system, we will apply the system-level parameterization (SLP) framework to convexly parameterize all controllers that stabilize the lifted model. The core technical challenge is to ensure that the resulting closed-loop dynamics, when projected back to the original state space, retain desired stability and performance properties, which we will analyze using Input-to-State Stability (ISS) tools and study the stationary distributions of the closed-loop system. The approach will be validated numerically on benchmark nonlinear control problems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel theoretical framework for convex controller synthesis for a meaningful class of nonlinear systems, bridging Koopman operator theory and modern convex optimization in control. It will deliver computationally tractable synthesis algorithms with formal stability certificates, extending the power of data-driven SLP methods beyond linear time-invariant systems.
proposals.novelty: While Koopman lifting for analysis is well-studied, its integration with the convex system-level parameterization for nonlinear *synthesis* is unexplored. The novelty lies in rigorously connecting the parameterized linear controller in the lifted space to a guaranteed nonlinear controller in the original state space, leveraging recent pre-2026 work on ISS properties of gradient flows and dynamic distributions.

proposals.title: Falsification-Validated ISS Certification via a Minimal Knowledge Scaffold and Active Learning
proposals.motivation: Certifying robust stability (ISS) for systems with significant dynamic uncertainty remains a core challenge in control. Traditional methods rely on strong, often unverifiable, model assumptions, leading to either vacuous or overly conservative guarantees. This gap is critical for safety-critical applications like biomedical devices or robotics, where models are inherently approximate and data is limited.
proposals.hypothesis: A framework combining a minimal, necessary structural assumption (a 'knowledge scaffold') with an active, falsification-based learner can generate non-conservative, empirically-validated ISS certificates for systems with highly uncertain dynamics.
proposals.method: We will formalize the minimal scaffold (e.g., a known control direction or incremental quadratic bound structure). An active learning loop will iteratively propose candidate ISS-Lyapunov functions from a parameterized class (e.g., neural networks or quadratic forms), using gradient-based optimization to satisfy Lyapunov conditions on sampled data. A concurrent falsification layer, using tools like SMT solvers or targeted exploration of the state-input space, will rigorously test these candidates, invalidating them if counterexamples are found and triggering safe updates to the candidate set and learning process.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new paradigm for robust stability analysis that explicitly ties guarantees to empirical falsification tests, reducing conservatism. It will yield concrete algorithms for assumption-light ISS certification, applicable to neural network controllers and partially observed systems, advancing the theory of data-informed robust control.
proposals.novelty: The tight integration of a falsification test as a core, driving component of the Lyapunov function synthesis process, governed by a minimal prior scaffold, moves beyond passive data-fitting or purely assumption-driven methods prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Discrete-Time Accessibility Criterion for the Algorithmic Synthesis of Event-Triggered ISS Controllers
proposals.motivation: Modern control systems increasingly rely on sampled measurements and asynchronous, resource-aware communication, necessitating controllers designed natively in discrete-time or event-triggered paradigms. The conventional approach of designing a continuous-time controller and then discretizing it can compromise stability guarantees and performance. A direct, constructive framework for verifying stabilizability and synthesizing robust controllers from a discrete-time system model is a critical missing tool in nonlinear control theory.
proposals.hypothesis: A discrete-time counterpart to Fattorini's continuous-time accessibility condition can serve as a constructive, algorithmic check for nonlinear stabilizability within a sampled-data or event-triggered framework. A positive verification from this check can directly yield a sparse feedback law and a discrete-time ISS-Lyapunov function, ensuring Input-to-State Stability.
proposals.method: We will first formulate a discrete-time accessibility/algebraic condition for nonlinear systems, inspired by Fattorini's criterion but operating on the sampled-data map. This condition will be developed into a symbolic-numeric verification algorithm using tools from computational algebra and distributions of reachable sets. Upon a positive check, the constructive proof of the condition will be leveraged to design an event-triggered feedback law, explicitly bounding inter-event times. The corresponding discrete-time ISS-Lyapunov function will be constructed using a stationary, iterative method based on the dynamic programming inequality associated with the sparse feedback.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified, algorithmic procedure for verifying stabilizability and synthesizing event-triggered ISS controllers directly from a discrete-time system model, bypassing the continuous-time design step. It will deliver a formally guaranteed, constructive method for co-designing sparse control updates and ISS-Lyapunov certificates, advancing the theory of robust control for networked and digital systems.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in fusing a discrete-time stabilizability test—a discrete analog of a fundamental continuous-time geometric control criterion—with the direct construction of event-triggered controllers and ISS-Lyapunov functions, creating a self-contained theory for sampled-data and event-triggered control synthesis.

proposals.title: Automated Synthesis and Formal Verification of ISS-Lyapunov Functions for Highly Nonlinear Systems via Neural Network Parameterization
proposals.motivation: Establishing Input-to-State Stability (ISS) for highly nonlinear systems remains a fundamental challenge in control theory, as classical methods relying on linear approximations or handcrafted Lyapunov functions fail to scale. The lack of automated, constructive procedures for nonlinear ISS analysis severely limits the rigorous certification of safety and robustness in modern autonomous systems. This gap between theoretical necessity and practical tractability motivates the need for a new, algorithmic framework.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a synergistic pipeline, which uses machine learning to generate candidate Lyapunov functions parameterized by neural networks and then employs formal verification methods to certify them, can provide rigorous, automated ISS guarantees for broad classes of nonlinear dynamics without resorting to local linearization.
proposals.method: The method involves two core stages. First, we will train neural networks to approximate non-quadratic Lyapunov function candidates by minimizing a loss function derived from the ISS dissipation inequality over sampled system trajectories and disturbance distributions. Second, we will integrate Satisfiability Modulo Theories (SMT) solvers or interval arithmetic-based formal verification tools to provide computer-verified certificates that the learned function satisfies the necessary inequalities over a compact region of interest, thus closing the verification gap inherent in purely data-driven approaches.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a fully automated, constructive framework for nonlinear ISS analysis, moving it from an analytical art to a tractable design-verification procedure. It will provide a library of formally verified, non-quadratic Lyapunov functions for benchmark nonlinear systems and establish a new paradigm for merging learning-based synthesis with formal guarantees in control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of neural networks as a rich parameterization for ISS-Lyapunov functions with subsequent formal verification, creating a closed-loop synthesis-and-certify pipeline that is not addressed by existing work on sum-of-squares optimization or purely empirical learning of stability certificates.

proposals.title: Input-to-State Stability of Highly Nonlinear Gradient Systems via Non-Smooth ISS Lyapunov Functions and Differential Inclusions
proposals.motivation: The Input-to-State Stability (ISS) framework is central to robust nonlinear control, but classical guarantees often rely on restrictive linearity or small-gain assumptions. Many modern systems, from neural network training dynamics to biological networks, exhibit highly nonlinear, non-monotone, and non-smooth interactions that violate these assumptions. A theory capable of certifying ISS for such systems without resorting to local linearization is a critical open problem in mathematical systems theory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ISS can be rigorously established for broad classes of highly nonlinear gradient and networked systems by constructing non-smooth ISS Lyapunov functions and analyzing their evolution via set-valued derivatives and differential inclusions, thereby circumventing the need for linear approximations.
proposals.method: We will extend the theory of non-smooth ISS Lyapunov functions, leveraging Clarke's generalized gradient and Filippov solutions for differential inclusions. The core method involves: 1) Characterizing the structural properties (e.g., prox-regularity, definability) of potential functions in complex systems that allow for constructive Lyapunov design. 2) Employing tools from monotone operator theory and set-valued analysis to study the differential inclusion describing the system's dynamics. 3) Developing a computational framework, based on sum-of-squares programming for semi-algebraic systems or numerical verification algorithms, to search for and validate candidate Lyapunov functions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, verifiable sufficient condition for ISS in systems with significant nonlinearities, directly applicable to the analysis of interconnected biological modules and non-convex optimization dynamics. It will extend the reach of robust control theory beyond the traditional small-gain and linearization paradigms, offering a principled tool for certifying the robustness of complex, high-dimensional nonlinear networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic fusion of non-smooth analysis and ISS theory for highly nonlinear systems, moving beyond the linear/small-gain context of Sontag's prior work on gradient flows. It proposes a constructive, geometry-aware Lyapunov framework that does not rely on local approximations or global monotonicity.

proposals.title: Mixed-Disturbance Input-to-Output Practical Stability: A Decomposed Framework for Nonlinear Systems with Composite Stochastic and Adversarial Inputs
proposals.motivation: Modern safety-critical systems, from autonomous vehicles to power grids, operate in environments with complex, heterogeneous disturbances. These include inherent stochastic noise and random failures alongside strategic, worst-case adversarial inputs like cyber-physical attacks or unmodeled dynamics. Pre-2026 theory largely treats these disturbance classes separately (stochastic stability or robust control), lacking a unified framework that provides explicit, quantifiable performance bounds for the composite case, which is essential for rigorous design and certification.
proposals.hypothesis: The performance of a nonlinear system under composite disturbances can be rigorously bounded by a sum of two distinct terms: an expected performance degradation due to the stochastic component and a guaranteed worst-case deviation due to the adversarial component, each dependent on clearly defined disturbance characteristics (e.g., moment bounds, energy constraints).
proposals.method: We will develop the Mixed-Disturbance Input-to-Output Practical Stability (MD-IOPS) property, extending ISS and IOS theory. The technical approach involves: 1) Formulating MD-IOPS using a combination of expectation and supremum operators over output trajectories. 2) Deriving sufficient Lyapunov conditions, requiring the Lyapunov derivative to be bounded by a sum of a term linear in the Lyapunov function (for the stochastic part, using martingale and moment bounds) and a term dependent on disturbance norms (for the adversarial part). 3) Employing small-gain and dissipation arguments to construct composite Lyapunov functions for interconnected systems subject to such mixed disturbances. 4) Demonstrating the framework via case studies on neural network-based controllers (linking to gradient flow stability) and biological network models (linking to single-cell 'omics dynamics).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified stability theory with explicit, decomposable performance bounds for systems with mixed stochastic-adversarial disturbances. It will yield practically verifiable conditions for controller and filter design, directly informing robustness guarantees for systems operating in partially hostile and uncertain environments. The framework will bridge the communities of stochastic systems, robust control, and network science.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit decomposition of the performance bound into distinct stochastic and adversarial terms within a single, coherent stability notion (MD-IOPS), which is not achieved by simply combining existing robust and stochastic stability tools. This provides a more informative and design-oriented characterization than treating the composite disturbance as a single, monolithic uncertainty.

proposals.title: Optimal Disturbance-Aware Proper Orthogonal Decomposition for Robust Reduced-Order Control of High-Dimensional Systems
proposals.motivation: Modern control systems, from fluid flows to neural networks, are governed by high-dimensional or infinite-dimensional dynamics. While model reduction via Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (POD) is standard, existing methods typically focus on capturing nominal system energy, neglecting the structure of stochastic or adversarial disturbances. This gap leads to reduced-order controllers that may fail to guarantee robust stability and performance for the full-order system under realistic uncertainty, limiting their practical application.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating a POD optimization problem that explicitly incorporates the statistical (e.g., covariance) or set-theoretic (e.g., energy-bounded) description of the disturbance process, one can derive a finite-dimensional control subspace that preserves crucial Input-to-State Stability (ISS) properties, enabling the synthesis of provably robust low-order controllers.
proposals.method: We will first extend stochastic and adversarial ISS Lyapunov theory to PDEs/very high-dimensional ODEs. We will then formulate a novel objective for POD that minimizes the expected or worst-case gain from the disturbance to a stability-relevant output, leading to a generalized eigenvalue problem involving the system's Gramians and the disturbance covariance or reachability set. This yields an optimal reduced basis. Finally, we will synthesize a finite-dimensional dynamic output feedback controller on this subspace and prove, using ISS small-gain or dissipation arguments, that the resulting closed-loop full-order system satisfies a quantifiable robust performance certificate.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, computationally tractable framework for designing certified robust controllers for large-scale systems. It will deliver concrete algorithms for disturbance-aware model reduction and establish new theoretical connections between nonlinear robust control theory and data-driven dimensionality reduction. The result is a rigorous bridge enabling the application of advanced nonlinear control techniques to complex, high-dimensional engineering problems.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the integration of disturbance characteristics directly into the POD basis construction for control, moving beyond energy-based reduction. Furthermore, providing explicit ISS guarantees for the full-order system under stochastic/adversarial disturbances when using a finite-dimensional controller synthesized on the reduced model is a significant advance over existing suboptimal approaches.

proposals.title: A Unified Functional-Analytic Framework for Input-to-State Stability in Coupled Dissipative Parabolic Systems
proposals.motivation: Input-to-State Stability (ISS) is a cornerstone of robust nonlinear control theory for ODEs, but its extension to infinite-dimensional systems, particularly coupled nonlinear parabolic PDEs, remains fragmented. Pre-2026, proofs are often system-specific, relying on intricate semigroup or nonlinear operator theory, which limits accessibility for applied scientists. A unified, structurally-grounded framework using elementary functional analysis is needed to systematically analyze complex systems like reaction-diffusion networks and thermoelastic composites under disturbances.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a broad class of coupled nonlinear parabolic systems admits a unified ISS proof via energy methods, contingent on a minimal set of verifiable structural conditions on the coupling, dissipativity, and external input channels.
proposals.method: We will first abstract common structures from key application domains (e.g., MHD, thermoelasticity) to define a canonical class of systems. The core technical approach involves constructing tailored Lyapunov functionals as weighted sums of subsystem energies. Using energy estimates, Gronwall inequalities, and Sobolev embeddings, we will derive sufficient conditions—expressed as inequalities on coupling strengths and dissipative coefficients—for ISS in L^2 and H^1 norms. The framework's efficacy will be demonstrated by applying it to case studies of coupled reaction-diffusion-advection systems with non-cooperative kinetics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a reusable 'recipe' for establishing ISS in complex PDE systems using only foundational analysis, significantly lowering the barrier to rigorous stability analysis. It will systematically expand the catalog of systems with proven ISS bounds and offer new insights into how coupling structures affect robust performance.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in distilling a unified, structurally minimal proof strategy that bypasses advanced infinite-dimensional operator theory, making ISS analysis broadly accessible while formally unifying previously disparate application-specific results.

proposals.title: Graph-Theoretic Conditions for Exact Controllability of Infinite-Dimensional Network Systems with Unbounded Nodal Actuation
proposals.motivation: Modern large-scale systems, from smart power grids to multi-agent formations, are modeled as stable dynamical networks where control input is applied at specific, localized nodes or edges, leading to mathematically unbounded control operators. Pre-2026, the functional-analytic theory for such systems (e.g., using Fattorini's criterion) and network controllability metrics (e.g., based on graph spectra and connectivity) have largely developed in parallel. A unified framework is needed to derive verifiable, architecture-aware controllability conditions for resilient design.
proposals.hypothesis: The exact controllability of a stable, infinite-dimensional network system with unbounded nodal actuation can be characterized by a precise interplay between the spectral properties of the network's graph Laplacian (or adjacency operator) and the analytic properties of the control operator's unboundedness, quantified through its degree relative to the system's generator.
proposals.method: We will model the large-scale system as an abstract Cauchy problem on a product Hilbert space, where the system operator A incorporates the network's interconnection structure (e.g., as a graph operator), and the input operator B is unbounded, reflecting localized actuation. The core technical approach involves: 1) Applying and extending Fattorini's Hautus-type criterion for exact controllability in this networked context. 2) Decomposing the spectral problem using the graph's eigenstructure to translate the abstract condition into a set of rank conditions on modal subsystems. 3) Relating the fulfillment of these conditions to network-theoretic measures such as algebraic connectivity, controllability Gramian-based centrality of actuated nodes, and the asymptotic behavior of eigenvector components at actuated sites.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, computationally tractable set of necessary and sufficient conditions for controllability that explicitly link functional-analytic unboundedness to graph topology. It will yield principled guidelines for actuator placement to ensure network resilience and offer new instability results for systems where certain graph motifs (e.g., bottlenecks) preclude controllability regardless of actuator strength.
proposals.novelty: The synthesis of precise infinite-dimensional controllability criteria (Fattorini's) with spectral graph theory to handle intrinsically unbounded actuation in network models is novel, moving beyond finite-dimensional approximations or ad-hoc network metrics.

proposals.title: Incentive-Compatible Input-to-State Stability: A Co-Design Framework for Strategic Dynamical Networks
proposals.motivation: Modern interconnected systems, from decentralized markets to biological networks, combine physical dynamics with strategic agent behavior. Pre-2026, Input-to-State Stability (ISS) provides a rigorous framework for robust nonlinear control, while game theory models strategic equilibria. However, there is no unified theory to guarantee that the equilibrium of a game among dynamical agents remains robustly stable against external disturbances, limiting our ability to design reliable adaptive systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a broad class of dynamical games, one can define a notion of Incentive-Compatible ISS, where the network's equilibrium is both a Nash (or related) equilibrium and possesses an ISS-Lyapunov function, and that incentive structures (e.g., taxes, subsidies) can be systematically designed to enforce this property.
proposals.method: We will formulate dynamical games where agents' strategies influence coupled ODEs or difference equations. Using tools from dissipativity theory and ISS small-gain theorems for networks, we will derive conditions under which a Nash equilibrium corresponds to an ISS equilibrium. The converse design problem will be tackled via mechanism design, formulating constraints on agent cost functions (ensuring convexity and monotonicity properties akin to passivity) that guarantee the existence of an ISS-Lyapunov function at equilibrium, potentially solved via inverse game theory or bilevel optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal bridge between non-cooperative game theory and ISS, yielding verifiable conditions for robust stability in strategic networks. It will deliver a principled methodology for co-designing agent incentives and physical dynamics to achieve provable disturbance attenuation in socio-technical and bio-economic systems.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of mechanism design with ISS Lyapunov theory is novel; existing work treats stability and incentives largely in isolation. Defining stability properties that must hold specifically at a strategic equilibrium, rather than at a predetermined operating point, represents a significant conceptual advance.

proposals.title: Compositional ISS Synthesis for Large Networks via Relaxed Observability Gramians and Graph Dissipativity
proposals.motivation: Classical control synthesis for massive networked systems, such as swarms or power grids, often relies on exact observability conditions derived from PDE theory (e.g., unique continuation via Carleman estimates). These conditions are computationally prohibitive to verify at scale and structurally infeasible in systems with sparse sensing. A new, scalable framework is needed to guarantee robust stability (e.g., Input-to-State Stability) without requiring full observability from every node.
proposals.hypothesis: By replacing the classical unique continuation property with weaker, compositional conditions based on graph-theoretic dissipativity and relaxed local observability Gramians, it is possible to construct a network-level ISS Lyapunov function and synthesize stabilizing controllers for ultra-large-scale systems where traditional methods fail.
proposals.method: We will define 'relaxed observability Gramians' for subsystems, requiring only that observability energy propagates through the network graph according to a dissipativity interconnection condition. Using small-gain or dissipativity interconnection theorems, we will compositionally assemble these local certificates into a network ISS Lyapunov function. The synthesis algorithm will involve solving local Linear Matrix Inequalities (LMIs) constrained by the graph connectivity and sparse sensing pattern, enabling scalable, distributed controller design.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a theoretically grounded yet practically verifiable method for stabilizing massive networked systems, bridging the gap between infinite-dimensional control theory and large-scale network applications. It will extend ISS small-gain and dissipativity theories to settings with fundamentally incomplete observability, offering new tools for the control of swarms, distributed computing, and infrastructure networks.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the replacement of the exact, PDE-derived unique continuation property—which is monolithic and unscalable—with a compositional, graph-based condition on observability propagation, enabling stability guarantees where classical observability fails for most subsystems.

proposals.title: Inverse Robustness Synthesis: A Statistical Ensemble Approach to Characterizing Admissible Perturbations for Input-to-State Stability
proposals.motivation: In control theory, Input-to-State Stability (ISS) provides a powerful framework for analyzing robustness to disturbances and uncertainties. However, the inverse problem—systematically characterizing the complete class of perturbations under which a given nominal system achieves a prescribed ISS margin—remains largely open. Solving this is crucial for specifying tolerable operational environments for a pre-designed controller and for understanding the fundamental trade-offs in a stability specification.
proposals.hypothesis: By drawing a formal analogy to the statistical mechanics problem of finding microscopic ensembles consistent with macroscopic observables, we hypothesize that the set of admissible disturbances and parametric uncertainties for a given ISS specification can be characterized as a 'feasibility manifold' in a suitably defined perturbation space.
proposals.method: We will formalize the analogy: the nominal system dynamics and ISS Lyapunov function define the 'energy' functional, while the prescribed ISS gain and bias act as constraints on the expected 'response'. For systems with linear or gradient-like structures (leveraging Sontag's recent work), we will employ convex analysis and measure theory to describe the set of disturbance distributions (stationary or dynamic) that satisfy the constraints. This involves solving a feasibility problem via linear matrix inequalities for parametric uncertainty and constructing disturbance signal classes using dissipation inequalities, effectively mapping the thermodynamic 'phase space' to the space of admissible perturbations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, constructive framework for inverse robustness design, moving from analysis to synthesis of tolerable environments. It will yield new computational tools to specify disturbance bounds and uncertainty sets guaranteed to meet stability margins, offering a dual perspective to traditional robust control synthesis.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the transposition of the statistical ensemble concept from thermodynamics to control-theoretic robustness, framing the inverse ISS problem as a feasibility mapping on perturbation spaces—a perspective distinct from standard Lyapunov or small-gain based analysis.

proposals.title: Characterization of Admissible Disturbances for ISS of PDEs via a Refined Fattorini-Hautus Criterion
proposals.motivation: For PDEs with boundary or point control, the control operator is inherently unbounded, complicating stability analysis. While forward ISS synthesis for such systems is an active area, the inverse problem—determining the precise class of disturbances a given stable closed-loop system can tolerate—remains open. Solving this is critical for the rigorous co-design of robust controllers for fluid-structure interactions and thermally actuated systems, where model uncertainty and actuator imperfections are significant.
proposals.hypothesis: The maximal class of nonlinear disturbances and parametric uncertainties under which a PDE with an unbounded control operator retains ISS can be explicitly characterized by a refined, quantitative version of the Fattorini-Hautus (FH) test, which accounts for the degree of unboundedness and the system's spectral structure.
proposals.method: We will develop a spectral framework for abstract Cauchy problems with B in L(U, X_{-1}). First, we will refine the FH criterion to quantify the gap between the original and the extended state space, linking it to ISS Lyapunov function constructions. This will involve analyzing the resolvent growth of the semigroup generator perturbed by the feedback law. Second, we will employ a dissipativity-based approach, using the refined FH condition to derive necessary and sufficient integral constraints on disturbance signals. The synthesis will be validated on canonical examples like the 1D heat equation with Neumann boundary control and a coupled wave-plate system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic methodology for inverse ISS synthesis in infinite dimensions, yielding explicit disturbance sets for PDEs with unbounded control. It will deliver a practical tool for robustness co-design, allowing engineers to specify permissible operational envelopes and model inaccuracies directly from stability margins.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the standard ISS synthesis problem for unbounded operators, moving from a known controller to the unknown admissible disturbances, a perspective largely unexplored. The key technical novelty lies in the quantitative refinement of the Fattorini-Hautus criterion to serve as a precise delineator of stabilizability limits.

proposals.title: Carleman Estimates and Unique Continuation for a Stationary Stokes-Lamé System with Coupled Interior Sensor Dynamics
proposals.motivation: The development of active microfluidic and lab-on-a-chip systems requires integrating ultra-fast electronic/optical sensor-actuator networks within fluidic channels and deformable structures. This creates a coupled fluid-structure-electronics (FSE) system where sensor feedback and actuation occur at microsecond scales, effectively instantaneous relative to fluid and elastic dynamics. A rigorous foundation for observer and ISS-based controller design for such multi-physics PDE systems is lacking, as it hinges on first establishing observability and unique continuation properties from partial measurements.
proposals.hypothesis: For a stationary Stokes system coupled to a linear Lamé system (modeling a deformable boundary/structure) with interior coupling terms representing instantaneous sensor-actuator dynamics, a Carleman estimate can be derived. This estimate will prove that the full coupled state (velocity, pressure, displacement, internal sensor state) is uniquely determined by boundary measurements of velocity and traction, or by partial internal measurements of the sensor field.
proposals.method: We will formulate the coupled system as a stationary PDE with non-standard interface and interior coupling conditions. The core technical approach is to derive a Carleman estimate tailored to this system. This involves constructing a suitable weight function, handling the coupling terms through careful energy estimates and interpolation inequalities, and accounting for the pressure term via the use of Bogovskii-type operators. The analysis will extend known techniques for the Stokes and Lamé systems individually, adapting methods from the study of fluid-structure interaction and elliptic systems with lower-order perturbations to manage the ultra-fast (effectively algebraic) sensor coupling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous unique continuation and observability result for a PDE model of integrated fluid-structure systems with embedded, ultra-fast networks. It will deliver a concrete Carleman estimate, enabling the design of observers and the analysis of input-to-state stability for such coupled systems. This bridges a critical gap between abstract control theory for PDEs and the engineering of reliably controllable micro-scale biomedical and chemical processing devices.
proposals.novelty: While Carleman estimates exist for Stokes and elasticity separately, and for some moving-boundary fluid-structure models, the inclusion of instantaneous interior coupling terms representing sensor-actuator dynamics within a stationary coupled Stokes-Lamé framework is novel. The challenge lies in proving unique continuation across physical domains when the coupling is effectively algebraic and acts as an interior perturbation, a scenario not covered by existing theory.

proposals.title: Control-Oriented Piecewise-Linear Approximation of Nonlinear Systems: A Graph-Based Synthesis Framework
proposals.motivation: The synthesis of controllers for general nonlinear systems remains a core challenge in systems theory. While piecewise-linear (PL) models offer a tractable framework for analysis and control design, existing approximation methods often prioritize state-space fidelity over the preservation of control-theoretic properties essential for synthesis, such as stability and reachability. This gap limits the reliable application of powerful linear/PL control tools to complex nonlinear dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by constructing PL approximations which explicitly preserve the gradient-like structure and omega-limit sets of the original nonlinear system, one can synthesize certifiably correct controllers (e.g., for stabilization or safety) using the approximate model.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage method. First, we will extend geometric analysis of gradient flows and omega-limit sets to inform a state-space partitioning algorithm, ensuring partitions align with the system's dynamic landscape. Second, within each region, we will formulate the PL model fitting as an optimization problem with constraints derived from control Lyapunov function conditions and ISS small-gain properties, building on recent ISS results for neural network gradient flows. This will yield a graph of linear modes where transitions are governed by the stationary distributions of the underlying nonlinear dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, control-oriented framework for PL approximation with formal guarantees, enabling the use of linear matrix inequality (LMI)-based synthesis tools for a broader class of nonlinear systems. It will contribute new theoretical connections between the geometry of nonlinear dynamics and the graph structure of their PL representations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly embedding control-theoretic invariants (e.g., stability properties of gradient systems, structure of omega-limit sets) into the approximation process itself, rather than treating approximation as a separate, purely numerical step prior to control design.

proposals.title: Lyapunov-Based Stability Criteria for Piecewise-Linear State Feedback in Hybrid and Switched Systems
proposals.motivation: Piecewise-linear (PL) feedback is a cornerstone of modern control, enabling efficient implementations of complex policies like those from model predictive control or ReLU-based neural network controllers. However, pre-2026 stability analysis for closed-loop systems under generic PL feedback remains fragmented, often relying on case-specific constructions or conservative linear matrix inequality conditions. A unified, formal framework is needed to certify stability for broad classes of hybrid dynamical systems governed by PL controllers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by exploiting the piecewise quadratic structure of the closed-loop vector field and its connection to gradient systems, one can construct piecewise Lyapunov functions and derive verifiable algebraic conditions that guarantee global asymptotic or input-to-state stability.
proposals.method: We will model the closed-loop system as a switched system where modes correspond to regions of linearity of the PL feedback map. The core technical approach involves: 1) Analyzing the geometry of these regions and their transition dynamics, drawing on recent work on stationary distributions and omega-limit sets. 2) Formulating the search for a continuous, piecewise quadratic Lyapunov function as a convex optimization problem (e.g., using linear matrix inequalities) by imposing constraints across region boundaries. 3) Leveraging gradient-like properties and ISS small-gain arguments for interconnections, extending prior work on gradient flows in neural networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic and computationally tractable framework for certifying stability of PL-controlled systems, directly applicable to verifying neural network controllers and explicit MPC laws. It will bridge the gap between classical switched systems theory and modern data-driven control design, offering new tools for safe autonomy.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the unified treatment of general PL feedback—beyond common saturation or dead-zone forms—and the explicit connection of its stability to the geometric and gradient-based analysis of the resulting hybrid dynamics, a synthesis not fully developed in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Scalable Piecewise-Linear Control via High-Dimensional Gradient Flows and Stationary Distributions
proposals.motivation: Piecewise-linear (PL) dynamical systems are fundamental models in control, neuroscience, and machine learning, but their analysis and controller synthesis suffer from the curse of dimensionality. Pre-2026, methods for stability and performance verification scale poorly with state dimension, limiting application to large-scale systems like multi-agent networks or high-dimensional neural circuit models.
