This work addresses the critical need for scalable, theoretically-grounded control frameworks for such systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The gradient flow structure of certain PL systems, combined with their ergodic properties, can be exploited to derive dimension-independent stability certificates and synthesize controllers using scalable convex programs defined on stationary distributions rather than the full state space.
proposals.method: We will extend the ISS analysis for gradient flows of neural networks with linear activations to high-dimensional, structured PL systems. Leveraging tools from monotone operator theory and the geometry of PL maps, we will characterize stationary distributions of the controlled dynamics. The core technical approach is to reformulate the control design problem as an optimization over these distributions, using scalable linear matrix inequalities (LMIs) and sampling-based approximations inspired by kernel methods, thereby avoiding explicit gridding of the state space.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel framework for high-dimensional PL control with verifiable performance bounds. It will yield computationally tractable algorithms for controller synthesis applicable to large-scale biological and engineered networks. The work will bridge gradient-based optimization, dynamical systems theory, and control, offering new insights into the geometry of high-dimensional dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in combining the analysis of gradient flow structures with ergodic theory (stationary distributions) to circumvent state-space gridding, a non-obvious synthesis of Sontag's recent themes. This moves beyond traditional Lyapunov methods to a measure-based, scalable control paradigm for PL systems.

proposals.title: Inverse Approximation for Piecewise-Linear Control: Characterizing the Class of Nonlinear Systems Optimally Regulated by a Given PL Controller
proposals.motivation: The approximation of nonlinear systems by piecewise-linear (PL) models for controller synthesis is a well-established technique in systems biology and control theory. However, the inverse problem—identifying the class of original nonlinear systems for which a given PL controller is optimal—remains unaddressed. Solving this would provide a fundamental understanding of the robustness and applicability limits of PL-based control designs, crucial for validating their use in complex biological networks.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given finite-dimensional PL dynamic controller, there exists a well-defined, non-trivial class of nonlinear control-affine systems whose optimal regulation (in a sense like infinite-horizon LQR or H-infinity) is achieved precisely by that controller, and this class can be characterized via a set of differential inequalities and stationary distributions.
proposals.method: We will frame the problem as an inverse optimal control problem. Given a PL controller, we will derive the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) inequality it must satisfy as an optimal controller for some cost function and plant dynamics. Using techniques from converse Lyapunov theory and ISS analysis, we will parameterize the set of nonlinear systems (drift and input matrices) and cost functions that solve this HJB inequality. The characterization will involve studying the gradient flow of the associated value function and analyzing the stationary distributions of the closed-loop system to define invariance conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic theoretical framework to determine the scope and limitations of a PL control law. It will yield verifiable conditions to check if a complex nonlinear system falls within the 'optimal domain' of a PL approximation-based controller, enhancing design justification in synthetic biology and neuromorphic control.
proposals.novelty: While forward PL approximation is standard, inverting this process to find the class of optimally regulated systems is a novel perspective. It uniquely combines inverse optimal control with the analysis of piecewise-linear dynamical systems and their stationary distributions, areas of established interest for the researcher.

proposals.title: Integral Input-to-State Stability of Bilinear Systems with Unbounded Nonlinearities: A Dissipativity and Gradient Flow Approach
proposals.motivation: Integral Input-to-State Stability (iISS) provides a crucial framework for analyzing nonlinear systems with inputs that are not necessarily bounded. Prior iISS results for bilinear systems often rely on restrictive boundedness or linear-growth assumptions on the state-dependent nonlinear term, limiting their applicability to systems with polynomial or more aggressive nonlinearities. Relaxing this assumption is essential for applying iISS theory to a broader class of realistic models in biochemical networks and controlled population dynamics where interactions can lead to superlinear growth.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a refined dissipativity analysis, leveraging the geometric properties of the drift vector field and its relation to gradient-like dynamics, can establish iISS for bilinear systems where the nonlinear term exhibits polynomial growth in the state, provided a suitable state-dependent scaling of the input is identified.
proposals.method: The approach will combine Lyapunov and dissipativity theory with techniques from the geometric study of dynamical systems. First, we will characterize the system's internal dynamics using a Lyapunov function derived from the gradient structure of the unforced system, building on Sontag's recent work on gradient flows. Second, we will employ a state-dependent input transformation to recast the system and construct an iISS-Lyapunov function. The analysis will involve studying the stationary distributions and omega-limit sets of the associated uncontrolled dynamics to understand the inherent dissipation, guiding the construction of a non-quadratic storage function that tolerates the unbounded nonlinearity.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the iISS theory for bilinear systems to a significantly wider class of nonlinearities, moving beyond linear-growth constraints. It will provide verifiable, less conservative criteria for stability, directly applicable to systems with multiplicative noise or nonlinear feedback. The results will bridge geometric systems theory and stability analysis, offering new tools for the control of complex networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically relaxing the boundedness assumption for iISS of bilinear systems by integrating geometric insights from gradient flow analysis and the study of omega-limit sets, a combination not previously applied to this specific stability problem. The proposed state-dependent input scaling method to handle polynomial growth is a non-obvious extension of existing iISS techniques.

proposals.title: Integral Input-to-State Stability of Time-Varying Bilinear Systems: A Dissipativity and Gradient Flow Approach
proposals.motivation: Integral Input-to-State Stability (iISS) provides a crucial framework for analyzing nonlinear systems with inputs whose energy is integrable but not necessarily bounded. Pre-2026 results for bilinear systems, where the drift and input operators are constant, are well-established. However, many applications in adaptive control, biochemical reaction networks, and switched systems involve parameters that vary explicitly with time, necessitating an extension of iISS theory to time-varying bilinear structures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a time-varying bilinear system can be shown to be iISS by constructing a suitable time-dependent, non-coercive storage function, and that this stability can be characterized through the dissipativity properties of an associated time-varying gradient flow.
proposals.method: The approach will leverage and extend Sontag's iISS dissipation theory. First, we will formulate the time-varying bilinear system and define a candidate storage function V(t,x), potentially derived from the solution of a time-dependent Lyapunov equation or a parameter-dependent energy function. Second, we will analyze the associated time-varying gradient flow dynamics to study its convergence and stationary distributions, linking these properties to the dissipation inequality. Third, we will employ a comparison lemma and integration techniques to prove that the system admits an iISS Lyapunov function, deriving explicit gain estimates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic iISS analysis for time-varying bilinear systems, extending the foundational constant-parameter results. It will yield verifiable sufficient conditions (e.g., on the parameter variation rate) for iISS and connect time-varying dissipativity to the geometric properties of non-autonomous gradient flows, enriching the toolbox for robust nonlinear control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the iISS framework for bilinear systems with time-varying operator theory, a combination not addressed in pre-2026 literature. The explicit use of the dynamics of a time-varying gradient flow to inform the stability of the driven system is a non-obvious methodological advance.

proposals.title: Converse Theorems for Integral Input-to-State Stability: Constructive iISS Lyapunov Functions via Finite-Dimensional ISS Methods
proposals.motivation: Integral Input-to-State Stability (iISS) is a crucial nonlinear control concept for systems whose unboundedness is integrable, not sup-bounded, generalizing ISS. However, constructing explicit iISS Lyapunov functions remains challenging, hindering analysis and design. While finite-dimensional ISS theory offers powerful converse Lyapunov theorems guaranteeing the existence of smooth ISS Lyapunov functions, their adaptation and application to the iISS case are underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: Converse Lyapunov theorems for ISS in finite dimensions can be systematically adapted to provide constructive methods for generating explicit, smooth Lyapunov functions that certify the iISS property for a broad class of nonlinear systems.
proposals.method: We will leverage the known relationship between ISS and iISS, where iISS can be characterized via a zero-output detectability condition and an ISS property of an appropriately scaled system. Starting from established converse ISS Lyapunov constructions (e.g., using integration along trajectories or Massera-type functions), we will develop an algorithmic procedure. This involves: 1) verifying the iISS detectability condition, 2) constructing an auxiliary ISS Lyapunov function for a transformed or scaled system using the converse theorem, and 3) integrating the scaling function to recover an explicit iISS Lyapunov function for the original dynamics. The method will be tested on canonical iISS examples from the literature.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, constructive bridge between the well-developed theory of ISS converse theorems and the practical need for iISS Lyapunov functions. It will yield new verifiable sufficient conditions for iISS and explicit function constructions, advancing the analysis of nonlinear systems with non-vanishing disturbances. The results will directly impact robustness analysis in biological and engineered networks.
proposals.novelty: While the existence of iISS Lyapunov functions is known, this proposal focuses on *constructive* methods derived explicitly from finite-dimensional ISS converse theorems, a connection not fully exploited in pre-2026 literature. The novelty lies in the algorithmic translation of existence proofs into practical construction tools.

proposals.title: Admissible Input Operators and Sharp Conditions for Integral Input-to-State Stability in Infinite Dimensions
proposals.motivation: The theory of integral Input-to-State Stability (iISS) provides a crucial framework for analyzing nonlinear systems with inputs that are not necessarily bounded in amplitude. However, existing iISS results for infinite-dimensional systems often rely on overly restrictive or ad-hoc conditions on the input operator, particularly for unbounded control actions. This limits their applicability to PDE models with boundary or point control. A rigorous, operator-theoretic foundation is needed to bridge infinite-dimensional control theory with nonlinear stability analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: By systematically incorporating the theory of admissibility for unbounded input operators—a cornerstone of linear infinite-dimensional systems theory—into the nonlinear iISS Lyapunov framework, one can derive sharper, more general, and practically verifiable sufficient conditions for iISS.
proposals.method: We will formulate the problem within a semigroup framework on Hilbert spaces. The core technical approach involves constructing a Lyapunov functional and analyzing the dissipation inequality under the condition that the input operator is admissible in the sense of Weiss and others (e.g., L^2-admissible). This analysis will link the admissibility property, quantified by its L^2-gain, to the required nonlinear dissipation rate in the iISS definition. The method will be concretely demonstrated on canonical PDE examples with boundary control, such as reaction-diffusion and damped wave equations, translating abstract operator conditions into checks on system parameters and geometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a formal connection between linear admissibility theory and nonlinear iISS, yielding checkable criteria for a broader class of infinite-dimensional systems. It will provide a principled method to determine when unbounded inputs are compatible with iISS, significantly extending the applicability of the theory to realistic PDE control models.
proposals.novelty: While admissibility is standard for linear well-posedness and ISS for bounded inputs, its explicit integration into the iISS framework for unbounded inputs—where the interplay between operator unboundedness and nonlinear dissipation is critical—remains an open and non-trivial synthesis of two major theoretical strands.

proposals.title: Inverse Optimal Control for Integral Input-to-State Stability: A Lyapunov Function Approach
proposals.motivation: Integral Input-to-State Stability (iISS) provides a crucial framework for analyzing nonlinear systems with inputs that are not necessarily bounded in magnitude but have finite energy. While forward iISS analysis and Lyapunov characterizations are well-established, the inverse optimal control problem for iISS—determining a meaningful cost functional for which a given iISS-stabilizing controller is optimal—remains largely unexplored. Solving this problem would bridge a significant gap between robust nonlinear stability theory and optimal control, offering a principled way to interpret and design robust controllers as solutions to meaningful optimization problems.
proposals.hypothesis: Given a nonlinear control system and a feedback law that renders it iISS, there exists a computable, non-quadratic cost functional, integrable over infinite time, for which this controller is optimal. Furthermore, the iISS Lyapunov function itself can be used to construct this cost functional explicitly.
proposals.method: We will leverage the known equivalence between iISS and the existence of an iISS Lyapunov function satisfying a Hamilton-Jacobi inequality. The core method involves formulating the inverse optimal control problem as the construction of a cost integrand such that the given controller satisfies the associated Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equation. We will employ a dissipation inequality approach, using the derivative of the iISS Lyapunov function along trajectories to define the state-dependent penalty in the cost. The technical steps will involve: 1) starting from a known iISS Lyapunov function, 2) synthesizing a cost integrand that renders the HJB equality hold, and 3) verifying the non-negativity and detectability conditions to ensure the cost is meaningful. The analysis will extend Sontag's 'pointwise' min-norm controller formalism and related dissipation-based constructions to the iISS setting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for inverse optimal control within the iISS paradigm, yielding a method to assign an optimality interpretation to a broad class of robust nonlinear controllers. It will deepen the theoretical connection between dissipativity, optimality, and input-to-state stability concepts. The results will offer control engineers a new tool for justifying and refining robust control designs via an associated performance metric.
proposals.novelty: While inverse optimality is classic for ISS and asymptotic stability, its extension to iISS is non-trivial due to the weaker, integral-type dissipation, requiring a fundamentally different construction of the cost functional that accommodates unbounded inputs with finite energy. The explicit use of the iISS Lyapunov function to derive the cost integrand represents a novel synthesis of dissipation and optimization.

proposals.title: Integral Input-to-State Stability of Finite-Dimensional Bilinear Systems via Gradient Flow and Dissipation Methods
proposals.motivation: Bilinear systems are fundamental models in control theory, bridging linear and nonlinear dynamics, with applications in biological networks and quantum control. While infinite-dimensional iISS (integral Input-to-State Stability) theory is advanced, its explicit specialization and verification for finite-dimensional bilinear systems remain underdeveloped. This gap limits the application of robust nonlinear stability analysis to a core class of practical models.
proposals.hypothesis: Finite-dimensional bilinear control systems, under appropriate structural conditions on their drift and control matrices, possess the iISS property, which can be certified by constructing explicit iISS-Lyapunov functions derived from an underlying gradient or dissipative structure.
proposals.method: We will leverage Sontag's recent work on gradient flows and ISS to identify bilinear systems that can be represented as generalized gradient systems. The core technical approach involves: 1) Characterizing system matrices that induce a stationary distribution interpretable as a potential minimum. 2) Constructing candidate Lyapunov functions based on this potential and analyzing their dissipation rates using matrix inequality techniques. 3) Applying the iISS superposition principle and nonlinear comparison lemmas to verify the integral-to-state gain property for the finite-dimensional dynamics, specializing the infinite-dimensional proof machinery.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a verifiable, matrix-algebraic criterion for iISS of bilinear systems, directly applicable to model validation. It will establish a concrete bridge between abstract infinite-dimensional stability theory and computational systems biology/control engineering, offering new tools for robustness analysis.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious element is the successful translation of infinite-dimensional, functional-analytic iISS results into finite-dimensional linear matrix inequality (LMI) conditions, explicitly linking the gradient flow structure of certain neural networks to the stability of general bilinear control systems.

proposals.title: Converse Input-to-State Stability Theorems for Non-Autonomous Nonlinear Systems
proposals.motivation: Converse theorems for Input-to-State Stability (ISS) provide crucial equivalences between ISS Lyapunov functions and the ISS property, forming a cornerstone of nonlinear control theory. However, existing rigorous converse results are largely confined to time-invariant (autonomous) systems. Many practical systems, from biochemical reaction networks to adaptive controllers, exhibit explicit time-varying dynamics, yet a formal extension of the converse ISS framework to such non-autonomous systems remains an open problem.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a broad class of forward-complete, time-varying nonlinear systems, the ISS property is equivalent to the existence of a (possibly time-varying) smooth ISS Lyapunov function, extending the seminal autonomous results.
proposals.method: We will employ a trajectory-based analysis, leveraging the system's forward completeness to construct a candidate Lyapunov function via a supremum of scaled norms along solutions, inspired by the classic 'Massera-type' constructions. Key technical steps involve carefully accounting for time dependence in the comparison functions (KL and K∞) and establishing smoothness through mollification arguments under appropriate boundedness assumptions on the dynamics. The approach will be validated by applying the theoretical framework to case studies, such as time-varying gradient flows and switched systems with dwell-time conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first general converse ISS theorem for non-autonomous nonlinear systems, closing a significant theoretical gap. It will enable the systematic use of ISS Lyapunov functions for analysis and control design in time-varying contexts, with direct applications to adaptive control, networked systems with scheduling, and biological oscillators.
proposals.novelty: While ISS for non-autonomous systems has been studied, a rigorous converse theorem establishing the necessity of a smooth ISS Lyapunov function under general conditions is absent. The novelty lies in the explicit construction and smooth approximation tailored to the time-varying vector field, moving beyond the autonomous or periodic cases treated in prior literature.

proposals.title: Stabilization via Vector Control Lyapunov Functions with Partial-State Feedback and Dynamic Observers
proposals.motivation: Control Lyapunov Function (CLF) methods, particularly vector CLFs for multi-objective and interconnected systems, typically assume full-state measurement, which is restrictive for real-world applications. Recent work by Sontag and others has advanced the theory of vector CLFs and ISS properties of gradient flows, but a systematic framework for stabilization with only partial, noisy observations remains underdeveloped. This gap limits the practical application of these theoretically powerful tools to systems like biological networks or large-scale engineered systems where full sensing is impossible.
proposals.hypothesis: For systems admitting a vector CLF, a dynamic output feedback controller can be synthesized using an observer that estimates the CLF's decay condition, ensuring practical stability or input-to-state stability (ISS) despite partial state information and estimation errors.
proposals.method: We will extend the vector CLF framework to the output-feedback case by integrating a dynamic observer (e.g., a high-gain or moving-horizon estimator) designed not just for state estimation, but to reconstruct the gradient information needed for the CLF condition. The core technical step is to formulate a joint observer-controller design where the closed-loop system's ISS property is certified using small-gain or dissipation arguments, analyzing the interconnection between estimation error dynamics and the perturbed CLF condition. The analysis will leverage tools from Sontag's recent work on ISS and omega-limit sets to handle nonlinearities and multiple attractors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a constructive method for designing stabilizing output-feedback laws for systems with vector CLFs, broadening their applicability. It will yield new sufficient conditions for ISS under partial information, contributing to the theory of nonlinear observer-based control and connecting CLF theory with estimation dynamics in a rigorous way.
proposals.novelty: While observer-based control is classic, its integration with the vector CLF formalism—where the observer targets the CLF's descent condition rather than just the state—is novel. Furthermore, the ISS analysis for this coupled observer-CLF system addresses non-trivial robustness questions not covered in standard full-state feedback CLF designs.

proposals.title: Quantifying Robustness Margins for Interconnected Nonlinear Systems via Vector Lyapunov Functions and Comparison Principles
proposals.motivation: Classical scalar Lyapunov theory provides qualitative stability certificates but often yields conservative or intractable robustness margins for large-scale or highly nonlinear systems. The vector Lyapunov function (VLF) framework, which decomposes a system into interconnected subsystems, offers a promising path to less conservative analysis. However, a systematic methodology to translate VLF-based stability results into explicit, computable robustness margins against model uncertainties remains an open problem in nonlinear control theory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging the structure of VLFs and the theory of comparison systems, one can derive computationally tractable, non-conservative bounds on admissible perturbations that preserve input-to-state stability (ISS) or other stability properties for interconnected nonlinear systems.
proposals.method: We will develop the method by first formulating a general interconnected system model with parametric and dynamic uncertainties. For each nominal subsystem, we will construct a component VLF, likely drawing on sum-of-squares or linear programming techniques for polynomial/linear dynamics. The core technical step involves analyzing the perturbed comparison system, using monotone systems theory and small-gain conditions, to derive explicit inequalities linking the VLF decrease rates to the magnitude of uncertainties. This will yield algorithms to compute robustness margins as the solution to a linear program or a convex optimization problem, validated on case studies like networked biochemical reactors or power grid models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, computationally oriented framework for quantifying robustness in complex systems using VLFs, moving beyond mere stability certification. It will deliver concrete algorithms for margin calculation and bridge the gap between abstract VLF theory and practical robust control design. The results will advance the field of nonlinear stability analysis by offering a less conservative alternative to monolithic Lyapunov methods.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit quantification of robustness margins—a *numerical* output—from the VLF framework, which has primarily been used for *qualitative* stability analysis. Integrating comparison principles with uncertainty modeling to produce computable bounds addresses a significant gap in the pre-2026 literature on vector Lyapunov methods.

proposals.title: Vector Lyapunov Functions for Scalable Stability Analysis of High-Dimensional Networked Control Systems
proposals.motivation: The analysis and control of large-scale networked systems, such as power grids, biological circuits, or distributed robotics, is hindered by the curse of dimensionality. Traditional monolithic Lyapunov methods become computationally intractable for networks with thousands of dynamically interacting nodes. A scalable framework is urgently needed to provide rigorous, compositional stability guarantees for such high-dimensional systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The vector Lyapunov function (VLF) framework, combined with modern computational and graph-theoretic tools, can yield tractable, scalable conditions for the global asymptotic stability of very large-scale interconnected nonlinear systems, even when direct analysis of the aggregate system is impossible.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational framework where each subsystem is assigned a scalar Lyapunov-like function. The interconnection structure will be encoded via a directed graph and a Metzler comparison matrix. We will leverage Sontag's recent work on ISS properties of gradient flows to construct appropriate subsystem functions for common network motifs (e.g., diffusively coupled systems). Stability of the full network will be verified by checking the sign pattern and spectral properties of the comparison matrix, a problem scalable to high dimensions. We will implement algorithms to automatically decompose large networks and construct VLFs from libraries of common component dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, computationally feasible method for certifying stability in ultra-large networks, moving beyond small-gain theorems. It will deliver explicit, scalable bounds on network size and interconnection strength that guarantee stability, and offer a software tool for automated analysis of networked system architectures.
proposals.novelty: While VLF theory is classical, its application to genuinely high-dimensional (e.g., >10^4 nodes) control systems with nonlinear dynamics remains unexplored. The novelty lies in the synthesis of this abstract framework with modern computational linear algebra and graph theory to create a practical, scalable stability tool for the era of massive networked systems.

proposals.title: On the Minimal Dimension of Vector Lyapunov Functions for Stability and Control
proposals.motivation: Vector Lyapunov functions (VLFs) decompose stability analysis into lower-dimensional comparisons, offering a powerful alternative to scalar Lyapunov theory for complex, interconnected, or large-scale systems. However, a fundamental gap exists: there is no systematic theory for determining the minimal dimension of a VLF required to certify properties like stability or input-to-state stability (ISS) for a given dynamical system. This lack of understanding limits the method's constructive application and obscures its fundamental expressive power compared to scalar functions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the minimal VLF dimension for a given system is fundamentally linked to the system's topological and dynamical structure, specifically to properties of its chain-recurrent sets and the geometry of its omega-limit sets, and that this dimension can be algorithmically bounded for broad classes of nonlinear and control systems.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined theoretical and computational approach. First, we will leverage tools from Conley index theory and the geometry of multiple omega-limit sets to derive necessary conditions on VLF dimension. Second, we will formulate the search for a minimal-dimension VLF as an optimization problem over the space of candidate comparison systems, using sum-of-squares (SOS) programming for polynomial vector fields and linear programming for piecewise linear systems. We will develop algorithms to iteratively test candidate dimensions, building on Sontag's prior work on ISS and gradient systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for quantifying the complexity of stability verification via VLFs, yielding concrete lower and upper bounds on the required dimension. It will deliver new, verifiable criteria for when a scalar Lyapunov function is necessary and sufficient versus when a VLF offers an essential advantage, advancing the foundational theory of stability certification.
proposals.novelty: While VLFs have been studied for decades, the question of their minimal necessary dimension for a specific system is entirely unexplored. The novelty lies in connecting this dimension to intrinsic dynamical invariants and creating an algorithmic methodology for its study, moving beyond existential results to constructive, complexity-aware analysis.

proposals.title: Controller Synthesis for Targeted Destabilization: A Convex Duality Approach via Vector Lyapunov Functions
proposals.motivation: While control theory predominantly focuses on stabilization, the deliberate and controlled destabilization of dynamical systems is a critical, understudied problem with applications in biological network reprogramming (e.g., driving a cell from a diseased stable state), robotic escape from local minima, and ecological management. Existing methods for inducing instability are often ad-hoc or lack formal guarantees on the resulting dynamics. A principled, optimization-based framework for destabilization is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the convex optimization framework used for stability certification via vector Lyapunov functions and linear programming admits a powerful dual formulation, enabling the systematic synthesis of controllers that render a target equilibrium or set unstable with prescribed characteristics.
proposals.method: We will leverage the linear programming approach for verifying input-to-state stability (ISS) and stationary distributions in stochastic systems, as developed in Sontag's recent work. The core technical step is to formulate the dual of these stability LP constraints, which will yield conditions for instability. This dual problem will be cast as a convex program to design a state-feedback controller that maximizes a spectral abscissa or alters the stationary distribution of a stochastic system, ensuring the target state is no longer asymptotically stable or attractive. The synthesis will be validated on models of gene regulatory networks and multi-agent robotic systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic convex-optimization-based controller synthesis method for guaranteed destabilization. It will extend the duality theory of stability certificates to a constructive design tool, contributing a new 'dual' perspective to the classical stabilization paradigm. The resulting algorithms will be directly applicable to problems requiring state transition in network systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in repurposing the duality of stability verification LPs—a tool for analysis—into an active design tool for instability, a non-obvious inversion of a foundational framework. This moves beyond heuristic destabilization to a formal synthesis with computable guarantees.

proposals.title: Lyapunov-Based Stability Guarantees for Hybrid Dynamical Systems with Stochastic Switching and Gradient-Like Subsystems
proposals.motivation: Hybrid systems, which combine continuous dynamics and discrete switching, are ubiquitous in modern applications from robotics to biological networks. Pre-2026, stability analysis for such systems, especially with stochastic switching rules and complex (e.g., gradient-based) continuous flows, remains fragmented, often relying on case-specific arguments. A unified framework providing rigorous, computable stability certificates is critically needed to enable the safe design of these systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by constructing a common Lyapunov-like function or a family of multiple Lyapunov functions whose expected drift along stochastic switching paths is negative, one can derive sufficient conditions for almost sure or moment stability in hybrid systems where the continuous dynamics possess gradient or ISS (Input-to-State Stability) properties.
proposals.method: We will model the hybrid system as a stochastic switched system, where the continuous flow in each mode is a gradient system or possesses ISS properties as studied in Sontag's prior work. The method involves: 1) Characterizing the stationary distribution of the Markov chain governing the switches. 2) Employing the infinitesimal generator for the coupled continuous-discrete process to analyze the expected time derivative of candidate Lyapunov functions, building on tools from piecewise deterministic Markov processes. 3) Deriving linear matrix inequality (LMI) conditions for the existence of a quadratic common Lyapunov function or a mode-dependent Lyapunov function satisfying a dwell-time condition under the stationary switching distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel set of verifiable stability conditions for a broad class of stochastic hybrid systems, directly linking the ergodic properties of the switching signal to the stability of gradient-like subsystems. It will extend control-theoretic stability tools to systems where switching is not adversarial but governed by a known stochastic law, offering a bridge between deterministic Lyapunov theory and stochastic stability.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit fusion of ISS/gradient flow analysis for subsystems with the stochastic analysis of switching governed by a stationary distribution, moving beyond deterministic worst-case switching or restrictive dwell-time assumptions common in pre-2026 hybrid systems literature.

proposals.title: Control Lyapunov Functions and Game-Theoretic Synthesis for Non-Strict-Feedback Systems via Immersion and Stationary Distribution Analysis
proposals.motivation: Strict-feedback form is a canonical structure enabling systematic backstepping control design, but many practical systems, including certain biochemical networks, interconnected mechanical systems, and neural network models, do not possess this triangular structure. Pre-2026, extending constructive nonlinear control techniques like control Lyapunov function (CLF) and Hamilton-Jacobi-Isaacs (HJI) solutions beyond this class remains a significant challenge, limiting the application of rigorous stabilization and robust control theory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combination of system immersion techniques and the analysis of stationary distributions for associated gradient-like dynamics can provide a pathway to constructively derive CLFs and solve corresponding differential games for a broad class of non-strict-feedback systems.
proposals.method: We will leverage Sontag's recent work on immersions and gradient flow stability. First, we will develop conditions under which a non-strict-feedback system can be immersed into a higher-dimensional system exhibiting a useful structure (e.g., a perturbed strict-feedback form). Second, we will analyze the stationary distributions of the gradient dynamics associated with the CLF candidate or value function, drawing on methods from the study of dynamic distributions, to inform the construction of a valid CLF for the original system. This will yield a synthesis procedure combining immersion mappings with modified Sontag's formula and game-theoretic controllers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new theoretical framework and constructive algorithms for stabilizing and robustly controlling important system classes currently outside the scope of backstepping. It will directly bridge Sontag's recent theoretical insights on immersions and gradient flows with practical control synthesis, expanding the applicability of CLF and HJI methods.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of immersion theory with the analysis of stationary distributions from gradient dynamics to circumvent the strict-feedback constraint is unexplored. This moves beyond incremental adaptations of backstepping to a fundamentally different geometric-probabilistic approach for control construction.

ideas.title: Adapting Lyapunov's Direct Method for Time-Varying/Nonlinear Operator Systems
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution approach from nonlinear control theory—specifically Lyapunov's direct method for stability analysis—to investigate whether a core result for linear time-invariant systems holds when operators A or B become time-varying or nonlinear. The analogous problem in dynamical systems involves proving stability without explicit solutions by constructing an energy-like Lyapunov function and analyzing its derivative. The adapted idea is to translate this framework into the original research context: instead of assuming constant operators, construct operator-valued or functional Lyapunov candidates that depend on time or state. The research would then determine sufficient conditions (e.g., slow variation, sector-bounded nonlinearities, or the existence of a common Lyapunov function) under which the core result's essential property (whether it concerns stability, controllability, or another system characteristic) remains valid. This approach shifts the challenge from directly solving the time-varying/nonlinear system to verifying the existence of a suitable Lyapunov-like structure that certifies the desired property.

ideas.title: Robust Unique Continuation for Nonlinear PDEs via Harmonic Analysis and H-infinity Synthesis
ideas.description: This research direction combines harmonic analysis techniques for proving unique continuation properties with robust control synthesis (H-infinity/mu) to handle nonlinear perturbations. The core objective is to develop a unified theory for the robust observability and stabilizability of complex stationary systems, where the unique continuation property ensures the state is determinable from limited sensor data, and the robust control synthesis guarantees the feedback law remains effective despite nonlinear model errors. This synergy enables the design of resilient control systems for PDEs where both precise state reconstruction and stability under uncertainty are simultaneously required, such as in advanced fluid flow or structural vibration applications. The result is a novel methodology for certifying that a practical, finite-dimensional controller can reliably stabilize an infinite-dimensional system based on imperfect measurements.

ideas.title: Study minimal finite control dimension

ideas.title: Inverse ISS Synthesis for PDEs with Unbounded Control Operators
ideas.description: This research direction addresses the inverse problem of Input-to-State Stability (ISS) for abstract infinite-dimensional systems governed by partial differential equations, where the control operator B is highly unbounded. The core objective is to synthesize, for a given closed-loop system and a set of desired ISS stability margins, the complete class of admissible nonlinear disturbances, parametric uncertainties, and actuator imperfections that the system can tolerate. Achieving this requires a precise, refined version of the Fattorini-Hautus criterion to accurately delineate the exact limits of stabilizability under such unboundedness. The resulting theory would provide a systematic tool for robustness co-design, specifying permissible operational environments and model inaccuracies for systems like coupled structures or fluid flows with boundary control.

ideas.title: Robust Reduced-Order Control for High-Dimensional Systems with Stochastic/Adversarial Disturbances
ideas.description: This research direction integrates stochastic/adversarial Input-to-State Stability (ISS) theory with model reduction via Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (POD) to develop a principled control design methodology for infinite-dimensional or very high-dimensional systems. The core is to derive optimal finite-dimensional control subspaces through POD that explicitly account for the statistical or worst-case properties of the disturbance process, ensuring the reduced-order model preserves crucial stability guarantees against the original disturbance types. This enables the synthesis of low-complexity, implementable controllers (e.g., finite-dimensional observers and feedback laws) with provable robust performance certificates for the full-order system. The result is a novel bridge between nonlinear robust control theory under uncertainty and computational methods for large-scale systems.

ideas.title: Nonlinear Robust Control Synthesis via HJI or Feedback Linearization for Guaranteed Stability
ideas.description: This approach directly synthesizes a robust controller for nonlinear systems by solving the Hamilton-Jacobi-Isaacs (HJI) inequality, providing a rigorous nonlinear generalization of H-infinity control with guaranteed disturbance attenuation and stability. Alternatively, it employs exact feedback linearization to transform the nonlinear system into a linear one in new coordinates, then applies mu-synthesis to the resulting linear model with its well-defined uncertainty structure. This method ensures robustness guarantees are valid across the entire operating envelope addressed by the linearization transform, not just near a single equilibrium point. The result is a controller that maintains performance and stability for the original nonlinear plant under significant perturbations and model uncertainties.

ideas.title: Computational procedure for nonlinear stabilizability analysis and constructive controller synthesis
ideas.description: This research develops a unified computational procedure that first checks Fattorini's necessary condition for nonlinear stabilizability using symbolic-numeric methods. If satisfied, the system automatically constructs a control Lyapunov function via sum-of-squares programming or linear programming for polynomial systems, or via neural network parameterization for non-polynomial systems. The procedure then synthesizes a continuous stabilizing feedback law using constructive formulas (e.g., Sontag's formula) or solves a pointwise optimization problem, followed by formal verification of closed-loop stability margins. The output is either a proof of non-stabilizability or a verified stabilizing controller with an associated Lyapunov certificate.

ideas.title: Automated Synthesis and Formal Verification of ISS-Lyapunov Functions for Highly Nonlinear Systems
ideas.description: This research develops an automated, constructive framework for deriving Input-to-State Stability (ISS) guarantees for highly nonlinear systems without linear approximations. The core methodology uses machine learning to synthesize candidate non-quadratic Lyapunov functions that respect the system's nonlinear structure. To overcome the verification gap, the framework integrates formal methods, providing rigorous, computer-verified certificates that the learned functions satisfy the necessary dissipation inequalities over a user-specified operating region. This results in a robust, algorithmic pipeline that transforms the theoretical goal of nonlinear ISS analysis into a tractable and automated design-verification procedure.

ideas.title: Network-Theoretic Controllability of Large-Scale Systems with Highly Unbounded Actuation
ideas.description: This research synthesizes network science and the precise analysis of unbounded control operators to develop a novel framework for the controllability of large-scale interconnected stable systems, such as those on the ISS or in smart infrastructure. It applies and extends precise functional-analytic criteria, like Fattorini's, to graph-based models where the 'B' operator's unboundedness is intrinsic to the network's actuation topology (e.g., control at specific nodes or edges). The synergy enables the derivation of necessary and sufficient conditions for exact controllability that are expressed in terms of network robustness measures, spectral graph properties, and the interplay with the unboundedness degree of the actuators. This provides a principled methodology to assess and design resilient control architectures for complex engineered systems where actuation is inherently localized or singular.

ideas.title: A Unified Framework for Input-to-State Stability in Coupled Nonlinear Parabolic Systems via Accessible Functional Analysis
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the goals of mathematical accessibility and broad applicability to develop a unified framework for establishing Input-to-State Stability (ISS) in coupled nonlinear parabolic PDE systems, such as reaction-diffusion-advection systems, thermoelasticity, and complex fluid-structure interactions. The core innovation is to identify and formalize a minimal set of common structural conditions—like specific forms of coupling and dissipativity—that, when satisfied, guarantee ISS using only foundational tools from functional analysis (e.g., energy estimates, Gronwall inequalities, and basic Sobolev embeddings). By abstracting the proof techniques from specific applications like MHD, the framework provides a reusable 'recipe' that makes advanced stability analysis accessible to a wider audience of applied scientists and engineers, while systematically expanding the catalog of systems for which rigorous ISS bounds can be efficiently derived.

ideas.title: Compositional Stabilization of Massive Networks via Relaxed Observability Gramians
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for the scalable synthesis of Input-to-State Stable (ISS) controllers for massive networked systems by introducing and exploiting relaxed, compositional substitutes for the unique continuation property. Instead of requiring exact observability from every subsystem—a condition derived from Carleman inequalities that becomes prohibitive to check in internet-scale or swarm settings—we define weaker, graph-theoretic and dissipativity-based conditions on local agent interactions and sparse sensing. These conditions allow the construction of a network-level ISS Lyapunov function through interconnection theorems, guaranteeing stability even when traditional unique continuation fails for large portions of the network. The result is a practically verifiable pathway to stabilize ultra-large-scale systems where classical PDE-inspired control theory breaks down due to scalability constraints.

ideas.title: Quantified Stability for Systems with Mixed Stochastic and Adversarial Disturbances
ideas.description: This research develops a rigorous framework for nonlinear systems subject to composite disturbances containing both stochastic (e.g., noise, random failures) and adversarial (e.g., worst-case attacks, unmodeled dynamics) components. It introduces a new stability notion, Mixed-Disturbance Input-to-Output Practical Stability (MD-IOPS), which provides explicit, quantifiable bounds on system performance. These bounds decompose into a stochastic expectation term and a robust worst-case term, each dependent on clearly defined disturbance characteristics like moment bounds and energy constraints. The result is a practically useful theory that informs robust controller and filter design for safety-critical systems operating in uncertain and partially hostile environments.

ideas.title: Apply Lyapunov converse theorems from finite dimensions

ideas.title: Inverse Robustness Synthesis via Thermodynamic Feasibility Mapping
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to a classic problem in thermodynamics and statistical mechanics—finding the set of microscopic states consistent with a given macroscopic property—to the inverse ISS problem in control theory. In the analogy, the desired stability margins (gain, bias) are the macroscopic constraints, analogous to fixed temperature or pressure. The analogous solution involves characterizing the statistical ensemble of microstates that satisfy these constraints. Adapted to this context, the research will develop a framework to synthesize the complete class of admissible disturbances (e.g., bounds, spectral properties, temporal correlations) and sets of uncertain system parameters for which a given nominal system possesses exactly the prescribed ISS margins. This transforms robustness analysis into an inverse design and feasibility problem on the space of perturbations, providing a systematic way to specify tolerable environments for a given controller or to understand the robustness trade-offs inherent in a stability specification.

ideas.title: Construct explicit finite-dimensional feedback laws via model order reduction and gain scheduling from aerospace control
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a standard solution from aerospace flight control to the problem of constructing explicit finite-dimensional feedback laws for stabilized parabolic systems. The analogous problem in aerospace involves controlling high-fidelity, nonlinear aircraft models across different flight conditions; it is solved by first using model order reduction to create low-order linear models and then designing explicit linear controllers (like LQR gains) for these models, often implementing them in a gain-scheduled fashion. Adapted to the control of parabolic PDEs, the idea is to rigorously apply model reduction techniques (such as balanced truncation tailored for parabolic systems) to derive a low-dimensional ordinary differential equation approximation that captures the dominant, stabilized dynamics. For this reduced model, an explicit finite-dimensional state-feedback law is computed. The final step is to parameterize this controller, making it an explicit, computable function of relevant system parameters or equilibrium profiles, thereby yielding a concrete, implementable feedback law for the original infinite-dimensional system.

ideas.title: Computational Pathway Analysis for Systematic Nonlinear Stabilizability Verification
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the systematic procedure for checking nonlinear stabilizability via Fattorini's criterion with computational biology methods for sensitivity analysis and modular decomposition. The core innovation is to treat the Lie algebraic conditions of Fattorini's criterion not just as a binary check, but as a 'control network' whose structure and sensitivity can be analyzed using algorithms from pathway analysis. This allows the procedure to not only determine stabilizability but also to identify which subsystems or Lie bracket interactions are the critical bottlenecks, providing diagnostic feedback for controller redesign. The result is a formally grounded yet computationally tractable tool for the analysis and design of complex, high-dimensional nonlinear control systems.

ideas.title: Nonlinear ISS via Bounded Eigenspace Geometry
ideas.description: This research investigates how imposing explicit bounds on the geometric multiplicities of the linearization's eigenvalues provides a novel structural constraint for analyzing highly nonlinear systems. This constraint limits the potential complexity of the center/unstable eigenspaces, allowing the derivation of ISS guarantees through nonlinear Lyapunov or ISS-small-gain theorems that bypass linear approximations. The core synergy is that the bounded geometric multiplicity assumption acts as a bridge, translating a manageable piece of linear spectral data into a powerful tool for constraining nonlinear system behavior, enabling stability analysis for a class of systems where traditional linearization-based or small-gain methods fail.

ideas.title: Unique Continuation and Observability for Ultra-Fast Fluid-Structure-Electronics Systems
ideas.description: This research direction aims to establish the unique continuation properties and Carleman estimates for stationary Stokes-type systems that model the coupled dynamics of fluids, deformable structures, and embedded ultra-fast electronic or optical sensor-actuator networks operating at microsecond or nanosecond timescales. The core challenge is to prove that, despite the extreme timescales and negligible delays creating effectively instantaneous feedback, the system's full state can be uniquely determined from boundary or partial internal measurements, a prerequisite for rigorous input-to-state stability (ISS) design. This bridges the gap between abstract control theory for PDEs and the practical design of next-generation lab-on-a-chip devices and active microfluidic systems, providing the mathematical foundations for their reliable control.

ideas.title: Assumption-Light, Falsification-Validated ISS Frameworks with a Minimal Knowledge Scaffold
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for certifying Input-to-State Stability (ISS) in systems with highly uncertain dynamics by combining a minimal set of necessary qualitative assumptions (the 'knowledge scaffold') with an active, falsification-based learning layer. The scaffold provides just enough structure to make the ISS problem well-posed, such as a known control direction or a bound on system complexity, preventing vacuous guarantees. The active learner then probes the system in real-time to construct and, crucially, continuously test candidate ISS-Lyapunov functions against observed data, invalidating them if they fail and triggering a safe update. This ensures robust, non-conservative guarantees that are explicitly tied to the system's empirically observed behavior, not just to prior assumptions or limited training data.

ideas.title: A Discrete-Time, Algorithmic Framework for Verifying Stabilizability and Event-Triggered ISS
ideas.description: This research fuses the goals of discrete-time/event-triggered ISS analysis and a systematic nonlinear stabilizability procedure. The core idea is to develop a formal, algorithmic check for stabilizability—based on a discrete-time interpretation of Fattorini's criterion or a suitable discrete-time accessibility condition—that operates natively on a sampled-data or event-triggered model of the system. A positive verdict from this check would then directly yield a constructive procedure for designing a sparse (event-triggered) feedback law and a corresponding discrete-time ISS-Lyapunov function, entirely within the discrete-event paradigm. This bypasses the conventional route of first proving continuous-time stabilizability and then discretizing the controller, creating a unified theory for verifying and synthesizing robustly stabilizing controllers for systems where only sampled measurements or asynchronous updates are available.

ideas.title: Feedback Stabilizability for Non-Analytic C0-Semigroup Generators: A Spectral-Resolvent Framework
ideas.description: This research develops a rigorous framework for feedback stabilization of linear infinite-dimensional systems whose system operator generates a strongly continuous (C0) semigroup that is not analytic, thus moving beyond the parabolic case. The approach classifies target systems by their spectral properties and semigroup type (e.g., hyperbolic, delay, certain coupled systems) and establishes necessary and sufficient conditions for exponential stabilizability via bounded feedback operators. The core innovation is a constructive design method that uses detailed spectral or resolvent estimates to synthesize feedback laws that achieve stability despite the absence of instantaneous smoothing, directly addressing the fundamental challenge of controlling wave-like or propagation-dominated dynamics.

ideas.title: Incentive-Compatible Input-to-State Stability for Strategic Networks
ideas.description: This research direction fuses equilibrium analysis and incentive design from game theory with the rigorous disturbance-to-state bounds of Input-to-State Stability (ISS). It develops a framework to analyze and engineer the stability of interconnected systems—such as social networks, biological regulatory pathways, or decentralized markets—where the nodes or subsystems are strategic agents optimizing their own objectives. The core novelty is defining and characterizing 'Incentive-Compatible ISS,' where stability properties (like ISS-Lyapunov functions) must hold at a game-theoretic equilibrium, and conversely, designing agent incentives (e.g., taxes, rewards, signaling rules) to ensure the resulting equilibrium is provably input-to-state stable against external perturbations. This enables a principled co-design of both the 'physics' and the 'economics' of complex adaptive systems for guaranteed robust performance.

ideas.title: Discrete ISS via Digital Signal Processing Sampling Theorems
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that ISS analysis must originate in continuous time. By drawing an analogy from Digital Signal Processing, specifically the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem, we propose a method to establish discrete-time or event-triggered ISS directly. The analogous DSP solution shows that a continuous signal can be perfectly represented by discrete samples if a condition on its frequency content (bandlimiting) and sampling rate is met. Adapted to stability theory, the research would seek to define analogous 'information sufficiency' conditions—perhaps based on the maximum rate of change of the Lyapunov function or the system trajectories relative to the disturbance—such that a discrete-time or event-triggered observation sequence can guarantee ISS without any reference to an underlying continuous-time model. The goal is to formulate a discrete-domain ISS theorem where the sampling or triggering condition plays the role of the Nyquist rate, inverting the traditional dependency on continuous-time analysis.

ideas.title: Deriving ISS Guarantees via Topological Data Analysis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the linearity or small-gain assumptions typical in ISS analysis by drawing an analogy from Topological Data Analysis. In TDA, persistent homology characterizes complex data shapes by tracking the birth and death of topological features across scales, providing robust, qualitative insights without linearization. Adapted to nonlinear control, the research would develop a framework to analyze the 'shape' of a system's dynamics—such as the topology of its reachable sets or attractors under bounded inputs—using computational homology. The persistence barcodes or diagrams derived from this analysis would then be used to define a new, geometrically intuitive measure of stability, leading to ISS guarantees based on the system's intrinsic topological robustness rather than on Lyapunov functions constructed from linear approximations.

ideas.title: Worst-Case Disturbance Design for Systems with Bounded Eigenspace Geometry
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the systematic design of worst-case disturbances to empirically test Input-to-State Stability (ISS) for systems where the geometric multiplicities of critical eigenvalues are bounded. The core synergy is to use the known bounds on eigenspace geometry—which fundamentally constrain control authority and performance—to inform the construction of disturbances that are optimally aligned to excite or destabilize these structurally sensitive modes. This leads to a new class of validation tests where the disturbance design is not generic but is instead tailored to probe the precise theoretical limits imposed by the system's algebraic structure. Consequently, the work bridges theoretical spectral analysis and robust empirical verification, providing a principled method to stress-test control systems against disturbances they are theoretically most vulnerable to, given their dimensional constraints.

ideas.title: Data-Driven Certification and Adaptive Control under Uncertain Geometric Multiplicity
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to close the gap between theoretical spectral assumptions and practical control synthesis. It creates algorithms to estimate bounds on geometric multiplicities from observed system data, complete with statistical or robustness certificates. These certified bounds then directly inform the synthesis of controllers with provable performance guarantees, even when the true system model is imperfectly known. The outcome is a robust, adaptive control methodology that is valid under realistic conditions of uncertainty and noise.

ideas.title: Interdisciplinary Case Studies for Unique Continuation via Harmonic Analysis
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the development of interdisciplinary case studies with the application of harmonic analysis for unique continuation. It will construct concrete examples where tools like Carleman estimates or quantitative unique continuation principles are used to prove that if a composite system (e.g., a cyber-physical network coupled to a social opinion dynamic) is externally stable (ISS), then the internal state in one subsystem (e.g., a compromised information layer) can be uniquely determined from observations in another (e.g., physical sensor outputs). This provides a rigorous mathematical backbone for cross-domain stability analysis, moving from abstract analogies to provable case studies on the propagation and detection of disturbances across system boundaries.

ideas.title: Relaxing Observability via Probabilistic Controllability
ideas.description: This idea seeks stabilizability conditions weaker than those derived from unique continuation theorems, which are often overly restrictive. The analogy is drawn from structural engineering, specifically performance-based seismic design, which replaces strict deterministic survival guarantees with probabilistic metrics of acceptable damage. The adapted solution is to reformulate the controllability problem by relaxing the unique continuation requirement—a deterministic, everywhere condition—to a probabilistic or measure-theoretic one. The research would aim to define new stabilizability criteria that only require the observation/control to be effective on a 'sufficiently large' subset of the domain (in a measure, capacity, or topological sense), thereby permitting failure on a negligible set. This could involve tools from geometric measure theory, probability, or the theory of capacities to quantify the 'size' of the uncontrollable subspace and establish stabilization results under these weaker, more practical assumptions.

ideas.title: Deriving ISS Stability via Physical Energy Analogs and Circuit Theory
ideas.description: This research expands the seed idea to formalize Input-to-State Stability (ISS) with minimal mathematical prerequisites by drawing an analogy from electrical engineering and circuit theory. The analogous problem is analyzing the stability of complex feedback circuits, which is solved using intuitive physical analogs like energy dissipation in resistors (serving as accessible Lyapunov functions) and fundamental input-output concepts derived from basic component laws and visual block diagrams, avoiding deep abstract mathematics. The adapted idea is to model the dynamical system's stability through physically intuitive concepts of 'energy' storage and dissipation, recasting ISS conditions as a requirement that the system's internal energy decreases relative to the input's supply rate—directly analogous to a circuit's power balance. This approach will derive rigorous stability results using primarily calculus, elementary differential equations, and visual/geometric reasoning via system block diagrams, making the theory accessible to a broader audience in engineering and applied sciences without requiring advanced functional analysis or intricate comparison function arguments.

ideas.title: Strategic Controllability in Infinite-Dimensional Systems with Unbounded Multiplicities
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the approximate controllability of systems governed by operators with unbounded geometric multiplicities, where the standard spectral gap assumptions are violated, posing significant technical challenges. It integrates tools from economics and game theory by modeling the control inputs as originating from a population of strategic, self-interested agents whose actions are governed by equilibrium concepts like Nash or correlated equilibrium. The core synergy lies in developing novel controllability criteria and control laws that are not only mathematically robust to the complex spectral behavior but are also incentive-compatible, ensuring that strategic agents find it optimal to contribute to the system's steering towards a target state. This enables the control of large-scale, decentralized systems—such as smart grids or distributed computational networks—where both high-dimensional dynamics and strategic behavior are fundamental constraints.

ideas.title: Constructive, Computable Hierarchies for Stabilizability Certificates
ideas.description: This project develops a framework to replace non-constructive unique continuation conditions with a hierarchy of computationally verifiable tests for stabilizability. It synthesizes tools from geometric control theory, spectral analysis of the Gramian, and data-driven approximation to create a decision process that can either produce a stabilizing controller or pinpoint the system's specific failure mode. The hierarchy ranges from fast, coarse checks (e.g., spectral clustering, controllability indices) to finer, more expensive ones (e.g., approximate nullspace analysis of the controllability operator), ensuring practical applicability. The output is a constructive certificate, making the theory directly actionable for control design and robustness analysis.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Stability Bounds to Control System Eigenspace Constraints
ideas.description: This research seed investigates how bounded geometric multiplicities impact control dimension and performance, drawing an analogy from theoretical ecology. In ecology, the bounded dimensionality of a resource niche spectrum limits species coexistence and determines community stability. The analogous solution involves deriving constraints from this boundedness to predict feasible stable states. Adapted to control theory, we treat the geometric multiplicities of a dynamical system's eigenvalues as 'stability niches.' The research will formalize how imposing bounds on these multiplicities (e.g., limiting the dimension of defective eigenspaces) constrains the minimum dimension and placement of actuators and sensors (the 'control resource') required for stabilization. Furthermore, it will derive fundamental performance limits—such as bounds on decay rates or robustness margins—that arise directly from these multiplicity constraints, establishing a theoretical trade-off between algebraic structure, control complexity, and achievable performance.

ideas.title: Adapt Noise-Cancelling Headphone Principles for Nonlinear Perturbation Rejection
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core principle of feedforward active noise cancellation from audio engineering to the problem of handling nonlinear perturbations in control systems. In noise-cancelling headphones, an external disturbance (ambient noise) is measured and an opposing signal is generated to cancel it out. The analogous solution is adapted by using robust control synthesis (H-infinity or mu-synthesis) to design a feedback/feedforward compensator. This controller uses information about the nonlinear perturbation (treated as a measured or estimated disturbance) to synthesize a control action that robustly maintains stability and performance specifications despite the perturbation's nonlinear and uncertain characteristics, effectively 'rejecting' its detrimental effects on the closed-loop system.

ideas.title: Stability-Certified Adaptive Model Reduction for Robust Control
ideas.description: This approach combines proper orthogonal decomposition for initial subspace identification with a real-time stability certification layer. The control system operates on the reduced-order model but continuously verifies local quadratic stability conditions. If dynamics drift or performance degrades, the framework selectively updates the POD basis or blends in a safe, stabilizing controller, ensuring the closed-loop system never enters an uncertified, unstable regime. This results in a control design that retains the computational efficiency of model reduction while providing formal robustness guarantees against model mismatch and evolving dynamics.

ideas.title: Structured Hierarchy Analysis for Generalization to Time-Varying and Nonlinear Operators
ideas.description: This research systematically maps the boundaries of the core result's validity by analyzing it across a precisely defined hierarchy of operator classes, ordered by increasing complexity (e.g., linear time-invariant, linear time-varying, Lipschitz nonlinear, monotone). For each class, the study identifies the specific mathematical conditions of the original proof that fail and determines necessary modifications or new sufficient conditions. The approach combines theoretical condition analysis with targeted computational experiments on minimal canonical examples within each class, ensuring findings are generalizable and reveal fundamental limitations rather than anecdotal failures.

ideas.title: Stochastic ISS via Statistical Mechanics Ensemble Methods
ideas.description: This research extends ISS theory to systems with stochastic or adversarial input disturbances by drawing an analogy from statistical mechanics. In statistical mechanics, the behavior of systems with vast microscopic randomness (e.g., a gas) is not analyzed via deterministic particle paths but through ensemble methods and statistical averages—using tools like Fokker-Planck equations and stability of probability distributions. Adapting this solution, we propose to redefine ISS for systems with non-deterministic disturbances by focusing on the stability of statistical moments (e.g., mean-square ISS) or the convergence of the system's state distribution. The goal is to develop Lyapunov-based conditions that ensure boundedness or convergence of moment trajectories, or exponential convergence of the probability distribution to an invariant measure, even when disturbances are stochastic (from an unknown but bounded distribution) or adversarial with bounded energy. This moves ISS analysis from a deterministic worst-case path to a statistical ensemble framework.

ideas.title: A Hierarchical Framework for Spectral Controllability with Singular Control Operators
ideas.description: This research constructs a precise taxonomy for unbounded control operators based on their singularity relative to the semigroup generator's resolvent. It develops a strengthened, quantitative version of Fattorini's criterion that is validated against each level of this hierarchy. The work explicitly identifies the spectral conditions under which the criterion holds or fails, producing sharp results for operators like those arising in boundary and point control of PDEs with weak damping. The outcome is a systematic theory that maps operator unboundedness to exact controllability thresholds, replacing vague 'deepening' with a structured classification and verifiable tests.

ideas.title: Approximate Controllability for General Coupled Nonlinear Parabolic Systems with Unbounded Spectral Multiplicities
ideas.description: This research direction aims to establish approximate controllability results for a broad class of coupled nonlinear parabolic PDE systems, such as those modeling complex fluid-structure interaction, thermodiffusion, or reaction-diffusion processes in biology. The core theoretical challenge is to develop new spectral techniques and observability estimates that do not rely on the assumption that the geometric multiplicities of the system's principal linear operator are uniformly bounded. This will require extending harmonic analysis and moment method arguments to handle operators with increasingly complex eigenspace structures as eigenvalues grow, thereby providing a unified controllability framework valid for a significantly wider range of physically relevant coupled models than previously possible.

ideas.title: PAC-Bayesian Certification of Dissipativity for Finite-Data ISS Verification
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to certify Input-to-State Stability (ISS) from finite, potentially noisy trajectory data by bridging PAC-Bayesian learning and dissipativity theory. Instead of directly bounding stability parameters, the method learns a probabilistic posterior over a class of storage function candidates consistent with the data, yielding a high-probability certificate of a dissipativity inequality. This dissipativity certificate is then algorithmically checked against a known small-gain condition to infer ISS. The approach formally accounts for approximation error and measurement noise, providing robust, data-driven stability guarantees for systems where a precise analytical model is unavailable.

ideas.title: Event-Triggered Input-to-State Stability for Networked Social and Biological Systems
ideas.description: This research direction develops a discrete-time and event-triggered Input-to-State Stability (ISS) theory specifically for the analysis of social and biological networks. It moves beyond continuous differential equations to model systems where interactions are inherently asynchronous and data-driven, such as opinion dynamics triggered by posts or cellular signaling activated by specific events. The core novelty is providing rigorous, disturbance-to-stability bounds for these complex systems using only sparse, event-based observations, without reconstructing an artificial continuous-time process. This enables the assessment of resilience in real-world networks like online communities or metabolic pathways under intermittent perturbations.

ideas.title: Controllability under unbounded resonance degeneracy via spectral clustering methods
ideas.description: This research adapts methods from quantum chaos to study approximate controllability of linear systems when the geometric multiplicities of the operator A are unbounded. In quantum chaos, the spectral statistics of systems with potentially clustered energy levels are analyzed using probabilistic tools like level spacing distributions, rather than deterministic bounds on degeneracies. The adapted idea is to reformulate the controllability problem by considering the spectrum of A as a point process and studying the controllability Gramian through spectral averaging techniques. Approximate controllability would then be established for 'almost all' operators within a suitable class, or under averaged conditions, even in the absence of uniform bounds on geometric multiplicities, by exploiting typical separation properties and eigenvector correlations derived from the statistical structure of the spectrum.

ideas.title: Spectral Clustering for Eigenspace Tracking in Nonlinear and Time-Varying Dynamical Systems
ideas.description: This research direction investigates how spectral clustering techniques can be adapted to manage and bound the effective geometric multiplicities and coherent structures in high-dimensional systems governed by nonlinear or time-varying operators. Instead of analyzing a fixed matrix, the method constructs a similarity graph from trajectory data or local Jacobians over time, applying spectral clustering to partition the state space into dynamically relevant clusters. This allows for the tracking of time-evolving modal structures and the identification of approximately invariant subspaces in regimes where traditional linear time-invariant spectral theory is inapplicable. The synergy creates a data-driven, geometric framework for complexity reduction and stability analysis in complex dynamical systems beyond the scope of either idea alone.

ideas.title: Learning-Based Runtime Assurance for Stability in Evolving Networked Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for stability in massive-scale systems by replacing static compositional criteria with a learning-based runtime assurance architecture. The system continuously observes local interactions and subsystem behaviors to infer and adapt stability contracts, using real-time monitoring to detect and mitigate emergent instability through corrective shielding. This approach ensures robust operation despite non-linearities and scale changes, providing practical stability guarantees for open, evolving systems like robotic swarms or internet services without relying on infeasible global models.
